channel.title: Green Curve Insights
channel.description: Daily ESG and climate compliance intelligence for Indian businesses.

item.title: Why Nature Matters: What Indian Business Leaders Need to Know About TNFD and the Coming Wave of Nature-Related Disclosure
item.description: The Taskforce on Nature-related Financial Disclosures (TNFD) has established a global framework that compels companies and financial institutions to assess, disclose, and act on their dependencies and impacts on nature — including biodiversity, freshwater, land, and ocean systems. For Indian busines
item.content:encoded: <h3>Executive Summary</h3><p>The Taskforce on Nature-related Financial Disclosures (TNFD) has established a global framework that compels companies and financial institutions to assess, disclose, and act on their dependencies and impacts on nature — including biodiversity, freshwater, land, and ocean systems. For Indian businesses, which are deeply embedded in nature-dependent sectors such as agriculture, textiles, pharmaceuticals, and infrastructure, this is not a distant voluntary exercise — it is the next frontier of mandatory ESG accountability. As global investors and trading partners increasingly align with TNFD, Indian companies that fail to understand their nature footprint risk losing capital access, supply chain contracts, and social licence to operate.</p><h3>What Changed</h3><p>In September 2023, the TNFD released its final recommendations — a comprehensive, science-based framework for organisations to report on how their business activities affect and depend upon nature. Built on the architecture of the Task Force on Climate-related Financial Disclosures (TCFD), the TNFD framework introduces the concept of 'nature-related risks and opportunities' across four pillars: Governance, Strategy, Risk &amp; Impact Management, and Metrics &amp; Targets. Unlike climate disclosures, which focus primarily on greenhouse gas emissions and temperature pathways, nature-related disclosures require companies to map their interactions with ecosystems — think water consumption from stressed river basins, deforestation embedded in commodity supply chains, soil degradation from intensive farming, or habitat destruction from real estate and mining operations. The TNFD also introduces the LEAP approach (Locate, Evaluate, Assess, Prepare) — a practical methodology for identifying where a company's operations and supply chains intersect with sensitive or degraded ecosystems. Globally, over 400 organisations have already signed up as early adopters, committing to begin TNFD-aligned reporting by financial year 2024–25. Regulators in the EU, UK, and Singapore are actively exploring how TNFD recommendations will be incorporated into mandatory disclosure regimes. India's own SEBI Business Responsibility and Sustainability Report (BRSR) framework is increasingly converging with global standards, making TNFD alignment a near-term inevitability for listed Indian companies.</p><h3>Who Is Affected</h3><ul><li>Large listed Indian companies (NSE/BSE) already subject to SEBI's BRSR Core disclosures, particularly those in high nature-impact sectors</li><li>Agribusiness, food &amp; beverage, and FMCG companies with commodity supply chains touching forests, freshwater, or marine ecosystems</li><li>Textile and apparel manufacturers dependent on water-intensive cotton and synthetic fibre production</li><li>Pharmaceutical and chemical companies sourcing botanical or marine-derived inputs</li><li>Infrastructure developers, real estate firms, and mining companies operating near biodiversity-sensitive zones or protected areas</li><li>Financial institutions — banks, asset managers, and insurers — with portfolio exposure to nature-dependent sectors</li><li>Indian subsidiaries of multinational corporations whose parent companies are TNFD early adopters or subject to EU CSRD requirements</li><li>Export-oriented businesses supplying to EU or UK markets, where nature-related due diligence obligations (e.g., EU Deforestation Regulation) are already in force</li></ul><h3>Key Obligations & Deadlines</h3><ul><li>TNFD Early Adopter Commitment (Voluntary — FY2024-25 onwards): Companies that have signed up as TNFD early adopters are expected to begin producing TNFD-aligned disclosures in their annual or sustainability reports for financial years starting in 2024</li><li>EU Deforestation Regulation (EUDR) — Enforcement from December 2025 (delayed from 2024): Indian exporters of soy, palm oil, cattle, wood, cocoa, coffee, rubber, and derived products must demonstrate deforestation-free supply chains with geolocation data</li><li>EU Corporate Sustainability Reporting Directive (CSRD) — Phased from 2025: Indian companies that are subsidiaries or significant suppliers of EU-regulated entities will face nature-related disclosure expectations flowing down their value chains</li><li>SEBI BRSR Core — Mandatory for top 150 listed companies from FY2023-24, expanding to top 1,000 by FY2026-27: While not yet explicitly TNFD-aligned, BRSR's biodiversity, water, and land-use disclosures are foundational building blocks</li><li>Immediate action (now): Conduct an initial screening of operations and supply chains using the TNFD LEAP methodology to identify high-priority nature-related interfaces</li><li>Near-term (6–12 months): Establish internal governance ownership of nature risks at board or senior management level, mirroring existing climate risk governance structures</li></ul><h3>Climate Transition Angle</h3><p>Nature and climate are inseparable. Approximately 55% of global GDP is moderately or highly dependent on nature, and the degradation of ecosystems directly amplifies physical climate risks. Deforestation reduces carbon sinks, increasing warming trajectories. Wetland destruction removes natural flood buffers, exposing assets and communities to intensifying monsoon events. Soil degradation reduces agricultural resilience to droughts — a mounting concern for India's food systems as temperatures rise. The TNFD framework is explicitly designed to work in tandem with the ISSB's IFRS S2 climate standard and the TCFD framework. As the International Sustainability Standards Board (ISSB) signals its intention to develop a nature-related standard building on TNFD, Indian companies that invest in nature disclosure infrastructure now will be positioned ahead of the curve when ISSB biodiversity standards arrive — likely within the next two to three years. For Indian supply chains, the stakes are particularly high. India is one of the world's most biodiverse countries and simultaneously one of the most vulnerable to nature-related physical risks — water stress, land degradation, and biodiversity loss. Businesses that fail to account for these dependencies are carrying unpriced risk on their balance sheets, a fact that institutional investors and credit rating agencies are beginning to scrutinise with increasing rigour.</p><h3>What To Do Now</h3><ul><li>Conduct a TNFD LEAP screening: Use the TNFD's freely available LEAP (Locate, Evaluate, Assess, Prepare) methodology to map where your operations and tier-1 supply chain intersect with ecologically sensitive locations — start with IBAT (Integrated Biodiversity Assessment Tool) and global biodiversity footprint databases</li><li>Assign board-level ownership: Designate a board committee or senior executive (Chief Sustainability Officer or equivalent) as accountable for nature-related risk oversight, and ensure it is documented in your governance disclosures</li><li>Integrate nature into your existing ESG/BRSR reporting process: Audit your current BRSR disclosures for biodiversity, water, and land-use sections and identify gaps relative to TNFD's recommended metrics — treat this as a gap analysis, not a separate workstream</li><li>Engage your supply chain: Issue supplier questionnaires or updated code-of-conduct requirements covering deforestation commitments, water stewardship certifications (e.g., AWS, FSC, RSPO), and land-use practices — particularly for tier-1 suppliers in agriculture, forestry, and raw material extraction</li><li>Monitor regulatory developments actively: Track SEBI's evolving BRSR framework, the EU Deforestation Regulation's enforcement timeline, and ISSB's nature standard development — assign a team member to produce a quarterly regulatory watch brief so your compliance strategy stays ahead of mandatory deadlines</li></ul><h3>Our Take</h3><p>The TNFD framework represents a structural shift in how capital markets will price nature-related risk — and for India, the implications are profound and proximate. This is not a framework designed for European companies alone; it is the scaffolding upon which global investors, banks, and regulators are building a new vocabulary of accountability for any business that touches land, water, or biodiversity. Indian corporates have navigated the climate disclosure learning curve with TCFD and BRSR — and those that moved early gained a measurable advantage in investor confidence and export market access. The nature disclosure curve will be steeper and more complex, because ecosystems are local, data infrastructure is nascent, and supply chain traceability in sectors like agriculture and textiles remains largely underdeveloped. But that complexity is precisely why the time to act is now, not when SEBI mandates it or when a European buyer sends a non-compliance notice. Companies that embed TNFD thinking into their strategy today will find it is not just a compliance exercise — it is a risk management and competitive differentiation opportunity that will define which Indian businesses remain investable in the decade ahead.</p>

item.title: Set a Target: Why Indian Companies Can No Longer Afford to Delay SBTi Validation
item.description: The Science Based Targets initiative (SBTi) provides a globally recognised framework for companies to set emissions reduction targets aligned with limiting global warming to 1.5°C — and validation through SBTi is rapidly becoming a baseline expectation from investors, global buyers, and regulators.
item.content:encoded: <h3>Executive Summary</h3><p>The Science Based Targets initiative (SBTi) provides a globally recognised framework for companies to set emissions reduction targets aligned with limiting global warming to 1.5°C — and validation through SBTi is rapidly becoming a baseline expectation from investors, global buyers, and regulators. For Indian companies embedded in export supply chains or seeking international capital, committing to and validating an SBTi target is no longer a voluntary 'nice-to-have' — it is a strategic imperative. Companies that act now will be positioned ahead of the compliance curve; those that delay risk losing market access, investor confidence, and social licence to operate.</p><h3>What Changed</h3><p>The Science Based Targets initiative (SBTi) is an independent body that validates corporate greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions reduction targets to ensure they are consistent with the pace of decarbonisation required by climate science. SBTi's Corporate Validation Services allow companies of all sizes — from large corporates to SMEs — to submit their emissions targets for independent assessment and earn the right to publicly claim their targets are 'science-based.' In recent years, SBTi has significantly scaled its validation infrastructure, introduced the Net-Zero Standard (requiring companies to address both near-term and long-term emissions across Scopes 1, 2, and 3), and launched the SME route to lower barriers for smaller businesses. Critically, SBTi is also developing sector-specific guidance — including for steel, cement, apparel, and financial institutions — making target-setting more rigorous and sector-relevant than ever before. The validation process requires companies to first commit publicly (signing the SBTi commitment letter), then submit targets within 24 months for validation against the latest SBTi criteria. Once validated, companies must report progress annually and recertify targets as standards evolve. The bar is rising: vague or incremental pledges are no longer sufficient — targets must be grounded in measurable, time-bound science.</p><h3>Who Is Affected</h3><ul><li>Large Indian corporates in export-oriented sectors: textiles, automotive components, IT services, pharmaceuticals, steel, and cement — where global customers are mandating Scope 3 supplier targets</li><li>Indian subsidiaries of multinational companies, where parent companies are already SBTi-committed and are cascading requirements down their supply chains</li><li>Companies seeking to raise capital from ESG-focused international investors, green bond markets, or sustainability-linked loans</li><li>Businesses subject to SEBI's Business Responsibility and Sustainability Report (BRSR) Core framework, particularly the 9 Key Performance Indicators (KPIs) on climate</li><li>SMEs supplying to large domestic or international buyers who are themselves SBTi-committed and under pressure to address Scope 3 emissions</li><li>Financial institutions and banks, for whom SBTi's Financial Institutions Net-Zero Standard is directly applicable to their lending and investment portfolios</li></ul><h3>Key Obligations & Deadlines</h3><ul><li>COMMIT: Sign the SBTi Commitment Letter via the SBTi website — this is the formal, public declaration of intent and starts the 24-month clock for target submission</li><li>DEVELOP TARGETS (within 24 months of commitment): Set near-term targets (5–10 year horizon, aligned with 1.5°C) and long-term net-zero targets (by no later than 2050), covering Scopes 1, 2, and material Scope 3 categories</li><li>SCOPE 3 ASSESSMENT: Conduct a Scope 3 emissions inventory to identify material categories — for most Indian manufacturers, this will include purchased goods and services, use of sold products, and logistics</li><li>SUBMIT FOR VALIDATION: Submit target documentation to SBTi for independent review; expect a validation turnaround of several months and budget for potential target revisions based on SBTi feedback</li><li>ANNUAL PROGRESS REPORTING: Once validated, disclose annual emissions performance against targets in public sustainability reports, BRSR filings, and CDP disclosures</li><li>RECERTIFICATION: Monitor SBTi criteria updates (particularly sector-specific pathways as they are released) and recertify targets if business activities or SBTi standards change materially</li><li>SME ROUTE: Smaller companies can use the streamlined SBTi SME pathway, which requires commitment to a set of standardised actions rather than a full bespoke target submission — reducing the resource burden significantly</li></ul><h3>Climate Transition Angle</h3><p>SBTi targets sit at the very heart of the global corporate climate transition. The International Sustainability Standards Board (ISSB) — whose standards India is actively considering adopting through SEBI — requires companies to disclose their transition plans and the emissions targets underpinning them. An SBTi-validated target is widely regarded as the gold standard for demonstrating that a company's transition plan is credible, not greenwashed. From a supply chain perspective, the European Union's Corporate Sustainability Due Diligence Directive (CSDDD) and the Corporate Sustainability Reporting Directive (CSRD) are compelling European companies to account for Scope 3 emissions across their value chains. Indian suppliers to European buyers — in sectors from apparel to auto components to chemicals — will face increasing contractual and reputational pressure to demonstrate science-based commitments or risk deselection. Physical climate risks compound the urgency. Indian businesses face material exposure to heat stress, water scarcity, and extreme weather events. Investors and lenders using climate scenario analysis (aligned with TCFD, now integrated into ISSB standards) will reward companies with credible decarbonisation trajectories and penalise those perceived as stranded asset risks. Setting an SBTi target signals strategic awareness of both transition and physical risk — and the intent to manage both.</p><h3>What To Do Now</h3><ul><li>CONDUCT A READINESS ASSESSMENT: Map your current GHG emissions inventory (Scopes 1, 2, and 3) against SBTi criteria. Identify data gaps — particularly in Scope 3 — and prioritise filling them before commitment. Engage a qualified sustainability consultant if needed.</li><li>ENGAGE LEADERSHIP: Present the business case for SBTi commitment to your Board and CFO — frame it around investor expectations, customer retention, access to green finance, and alignment with BRSR Core disclosures rather than purely compliance cost.</li><li>SIGN THE COMMITMENT LETTER: Do not wait for perfect data. Sign the SBTi commitment letter now to signal intent publicly — this also provides a 24-month runway to develop robust targets. Early movers gain reputational advantage in their sector.</li><li>DEVELOP AN INTERNAL ROADMAP: Assign cross-functional ownership (Finance, Operations, Procurement, Sustainability) for target development. Set internal milestones for Scope 3 data collection, target modelling, and submission — do not let the 24-month window slip by default.</li><li>ALIGN WITH BROADER REPORTING FRAMEWORKS: Ensure your SBTi journey is integrated with your BRSR Core disclosures, CDP submission, and any ISSB-aligned reporting you are preparing — creating a single, consistent climate narrative for all stakeholders.</li></ul><h3>Our Take</h3><p>Setting a science-based target is no longer an act of corporate voluntarism — it is the entry ticket to the next decade of global commerce and capital markets. For Indian companies, the window to act proactively is narrowing fast: SEBI's BRSR Core framework, incoming ISSB-aligned standards, and the Scope 3 ambitions of multinational buyers are converging to make SBTi validation a de facto market requirement across sectors. What makes this moment particularly significant is that SBTi is simultaneously raising its standards — sector pathways are becoming more stringent, net-zero criteria are tightening, and greenwashing scrutiny is intensifying globally. Companies that commit and validate their targets now will not only avoid future compliance scrambles but will build the internal data systems, supplier engagement capabilities, and credibility with investors that define resilient, transition-ready businesses. The question is no longer whether to set a science-based target — it is how quickly you can get started.</p>

item.title: Mastering GRI Standards: A Practical Guide for Indian Companies Navigating Sustainability Reporting
item.description: The Global Reporting Initiative (GRI) Standards remain the world's most widely adopted sustainability reporting framework, and Indian companies — whether driven by SEBI's BRSR mandate, investor due diligence, or global supply chain requirements — increasingly need to understand how to use them corre
item.content:encoded: <h3>Executive Summary</h3><p>The Global Reporting Initiative (GRI) Standards remain the world's most widely adopted sustainability reporting framework, and Indian companies — whether driven by SEBI's BRSR mandate, investor due diligence, or global supply chain requirements — increasingly need to understand how to use them correctly. Misapplication of GRI Standards is one of the most common and costly compliance mistakes organisations make, leading to greenwashing allegations, audit failures, and lost credibility with stakeholders. This post breaks down how to use the GRI Standards effectively, who needs to act, and what your sustainability team should do right now.</p><h3>What Changed</h3><p>The GRI Standards are a modular, interrelated set of disclosure requirements published by the Global Reporting Initiative, a globally recognised independent standards body headquartered in Amsterdam. The Standards were substantially updated in 2021, with the revised Universal Standards (GRI 1, 2, and 3) taking effect from 1 January 2023, replacing the previous GRI 101, 102, and 103 framework. This was one of the most significant overhauls in GRI's history. The updated framework introduces a clearer three-tier architecture: GRI 1 (Foundation) sets out the purpose, key concepts, and requirements for using the Standards; GRI 2 (General Disclosures) covers organisational profile, governance, strategy, and stakeholder engagement; and GRI 3 (Material Topics) provides a structured methodology for determining and reporting on material topics. These are then supplemented by Sector Standards and Topic Standards covering specific environmental, social, and governance issues such as emissions (GRI 305), water (GRI 303), occupational health and safety (GRI 403), and anti-corruption (GRI 205), among many others. A critical distinction that many organisations miss is the difference between reporting 'in accordance with' GRI Standards — which requires meeting all applicable requirements — versus 'with reference to' GRI Standards, which is a more flexible approach allowing selective use of disclosures. Both pathways are legitimate, but they carry different credibility signals to investors and regulators, and companies must clearly state which pathway they have followed.</p><h3>Who Is Affected</h3><ul><li>Top 1,000 listed companies in India required to file BRSR (Business Responsibility and Sustainability Report) with SEBI — GRI alignment strengthens BRSR disclosures significantly</li><li>Indian subsidiaries and supply chain partners of European multinationals subject to CSRD (Corporate Sustainability Reporting Directive), which references GRI as an accepted framework</li><li>Export-oriented manufacturers in textiles, chemicals, automotive, and pharmaceuticals facing GRI-based supplier assessments from global buyers</li><li>Private equity and venture-backed companies preparing for IPO or seeking ESG-focused institutional investment</li><li>Public Sector Undertakings (PSUs) and CPSEs increasingly expected to align with GRI under DPE (Department of Public Enterprises) sustainability guidelines</li><li>Mid-sized companies in the MSME segment that are part of listed company supply chains, as Scope 3 disclosure requirements push GRI expectations downstream</li><li>Financial institutions, banks, and NBFCs responding to RBI's disclosure expectations and investor ESG scoring frameworks that use GRI as a benchmark</li></ul><h3>Key Obligations & Deadlines</h3><ul><li>IMMEDIATE: Confirm whether your organisation is reporting 'in accordance with' GRI or 'with reference to' GRI — this must be explicitly stated in your sustainability report and GRI Content Index</li><li>IMMEDIATE: Ensure your GRI Content Index is complete, accurate, and publicly accessible — this is the primary audit trail for any GRI-based disclosure and is scrutinised by ESG rating agencies such as MSCI, Sustainalytics, and CDP</li><li>BY NEXT REPORTING CYCLE: Complete a formal materiality assessment aligned with GRI 3 requirements, including identification of actual and potential impacts across your value chain — the 2021 update requires 'impact materiality' not just financial materiality</li><li>BY NEXT REPORTING CYCLE: Map your existing BRSR disclosures against the GRI Content Index to identify gaps — SEBI's BRSR Core indicators align closely with several GRI Topic Standards, enabling dual-use reporting efficiency</li><li>ONGOING: Sector Standards (e.g., GRI 11 for Oil &amp; Gas, GRI 13 for Agriculture) are being rolled out progressively — companies in covered sectors must integrate applicable Sector Standards into their reporting as they become effective</li><li>ONGOING: Maintain documented evidence for all GRI disclosures — third-party assurance over GRI reports is increasingly expected by institutional investors and is mandatory for BRSR Core disclosures for the top 150 listed companies from FY2023-24</li><li>2024-2025: Watch for GRI's forthcoming Sector Standards covering financial services, mining, and infrastructure — begin gap assessments now if your sector is in the pipeline</li></ul><h3>Climate Transition Angle</h3><p>GRI Standards are not purely a reporting compliance exercise — they are a foundational tool for managing and communicating your company's exposure to climate transition risks and physical climate risks. GRI 201 (Economic Performance) now encourages disclosure of financial implications of climate risks, directly paralleling the recommendations of the Task Force on Climate-related Financial Disclosures (TCFD) and the newly effective ISSB Standards (IFRS S2). For Indian companies, the stakes are particularly high. India's geographic exposure to physical climate risks — extreme heat, monsoon variability, flooding, and water stress — means that GRI disclosures on water (GRI 303), energy (GRI 302), and emissions (GRI 305) are no longer just reputational exercises. They are signals of operational resilience that sophisticated investors and lenders are actively pricing. Companies that cannot produce credible, GRI-aligned data on these metrics are increasingly disadvantaged in capital markets. The convergence of global frameworks is accelerating. The ISSB's IFRS S1 and S2 Standards, now adopted or under consideration by regulators in over 20 jurisdictions, explicitly acknowledge GRI as a complementary framework for impact-focused disclosures. The European Union's CSRD mandates the European Sustainability Reporting Standards (ESRS), which were developed in collaboration with GRI to ensure interoperability. For Indian exporters and companies with European investors or customers, this means GRI compliance is increasingly a market access requirement, not just a voluntary best practice. Building GRI competency now positions your organisation at the intersection of every major global reporting standard that matters.</p><h3>What To Do Now</h3><ul><li>CONDUCT A GRI FRAMEWORK AUDIT: Map your current sustainability report against the 2021 GRI Universal Standards (GRI 1, 2, and 3) to identify structural gaps — many companies are still inadvertently using the old 2016 framework structure, which is no longer current</li><li>INVEST IN MATERIALITY ASSESSMENT: Commission or refresh your materiality assessment using GRI 3's double materiality lens, covering both your impacts on people and planet AND the financial risks and opportunities that sustainability issues pose to your business — this is the backbone of credible GRI reporting and increasingly required by BRSR and CSRD-linked stakeholders</li><li>BUILD YOUR GRI CONTENT INDEX: Create or update a structured GRI Content Index that maps each required disclosure to the relevant section of your report, indicates omissions with clear justifications, and is published as a standalone document on your website — this is the single most important credibility signal for ESG raters and investors</li><li>ENGAGE YOUR DATA OWNERS: GRI reporting requires granular, verifiable data from operations, HR, procurement, legal, and finance — establish internal data governance protocols so that sustainability disclosures are treated with the same rigour as financial reporting, including clear ownership, collection timelines, and audit trails</li><li>PLAN FOR ASSURANCE: Engage a third-party assurance provider familiar with GRI's assurance protocols to conduct limited or reasonable assurance over your key GRI disclosures — this is already mandatory for BRSR Core disclosures and will become a baseline expectation across your investor and customer base within 2-3 years</li></ul><h3>Our Take</h3><p>GRI Standards have quietly become the de facto common language of global sustainability disclosure — and Indian companies that treat them as a box-ticking exercise are making a strategic error they may not be able to correct quickly. The 2021 framework update was not cosmetic; it fundamentally shifted reporting from a communications function to an accountability function, embedding impact assessment, stakeholder engagement, and value chain thinking at the core of what disclosure means. As SEBI continues to strengthen BRSR requirements, as European customers demand CSRD-compatible supplier data, and as the ISSB framework gains regulatory traction across Asia, the companies that have invested in robust GRI infrastructure will find themselves with a significant competitive and reputational advantage. Those that have not will face a scramble to catch up under regulatory pressure and investor scrutiny simultaneously. The time to build this capability is now — not in response to the next enforcement notice.</p>

item.title: EFRAG & EU CSRD: What Indian Companies Must Know About Europe's Sustainability Reporting Revolution
item.description: The European Union's Corporate Sustainability Reporting Directive (CSRD), developed in close collaboration with EFRAG (European Financial Reporting Advisory Group), is rapidly becoming the world's most demanding sustainability disclosure framework — and its reach extends well beyond European borders
item.content:encoded: <h3>Executive Summary</h3><p>The European Union's Corporate Sustainability Reporting Directive (CSRD), developed in close collaboration with EFRAG (European Financial Reporting Advisory Group), is rapidly becoming the world's most demanding sustainability disclosure framework — and its reach extends well beyond European borders. Indian companies that supply to, invest in, or operate within EU markets are increasingly expected to provide granular ESG data to their European counterparts. Boards and sustainability officers who treat CSRD as a 'European problem' do so at significant commercial and reputational risk.</p><h3>What Changed</h3><p>The CSRD replaced the older Non-Financial Reporting Directive (NFRD) and dramatically expanded the scope, depth, and assurance requirements for sustainability disclosures across the EU. EFRAG, as the technical architect of the European Sustainability Reporting Standards (ESRS), has developed a comprehensive set of standards covering climate, biodiversity, water, social factors, and governance — all underpinned by the concept of 'double materiality,' meaning companies must report both on how sustainability issues affect their business AND how their business affects the world. What makes this directly relevant to India is the 'value chain' provision. EU-based companies subject to CSRD are required to collect and disclose sustainability data from their entire value chain — including non-EU suppliers, joint venture partners, and investee companies. This means that even if an Indian company is not itself subject to CSRD, it will face mounting requests from its EU customers and investors for ESRS-aligned ESG disclosures. EFRAG continues to publish implementation guidance, Q&amp;A documents, and sector-specific standards that shape exactly what data will be requested.</p><h3>Who Is Affected</h3><ul><li>Large Indian exporters to the EU across sectors including textiles, pharmaceuticals, automotive components, chemicals, IT services, and agri-commodities</li><li>Indian subsidiaries or joint ventures of EU-headquartered companies already subject to CSRD reporting obligations</li><li>Indian companies listed on EU exchanges or with significant EU institutional investor bases</li><li>SMEs in the supply chain of large EU corporates, who face indirect disclosure requests even if not formally regulated</li><li>Indian financial institutions and asset managers with EU clients or co-investment structures</li><li>Companies preparing for BRSR Core alignment, as CSRD/ESRS represents the global benchmark those frameworks are converging toward</li></ul><h3>Key Obligations & Deadlines</h3><ul><li>Wave 1 (FY2024, reporting in 2025): ~11,500 large EU public-interest entities already under NFRD — their value chain data requests to Indian suppliers are live NOW</li><li>Wave 2 (FY2025, reporting in 2026): All large EU companies (500+ employees OR €40M+ turnover OR €20M+ balance sheet, meeting 2 of 3 criteria) — significantly expands the number of EU buyers requesting Indian supplier ESG data</li><li>Wave 3 (FY2026, reporting in 2027): Listed EU SMEs — broadens the net further into mid-market supply chains</li><li>Non-EU parent companies with EU-listed securities or €150M+ EU net turnover and at least one large EU subsidiary or branch: subject to CSRD from FY2028 — directly capturing large Indian multinationals with EU presence</li><li>EFRAG sector-specific ESRS standards (covering oil &amp; gas, mining, retail, food &amp; beverages, etc.) expected to be finalised progressively — Indian exporters in these sectors should monitor EFRAG's publication calendar closely</li><li>Limited assurance on CSRD disclosures required from Wave 1; reasonable assurance expected by 2028 — internal data quality must improve now, not at deadline</li></ul><h3>Climate Transition Angle</h3><p>CSRD and the ESRS are not merely a compliance exercise — they are the EU's primary mechanism for redirecting capital toward companies credibly managing climate transition risks. ESRS E1 (Climate Change) is the most detailed and non-waivable standard, requiring companies to disclose Scope 1, 2, and 3 greenhouse gas emissions, transition plans aligned with the Paris Agreement's 1.5°C pathway, physical climate risk assessments, and carbon price assumptions embedded in financial planning. For Indian companies, this creates an urgent intersection with physical climate risk. India is among the world's most climate-vulnerable economies — extreme heat, monsoon variability, and water stress directly affect operations across manufacturing, agriculture, and infrastructure. EU buyers and investors now need to understand these physical risks within their own supply chain disclosures. Indian companies that can quantify and communicate their climate resilience will be commercially advantaged over those that cannot. The CSRD's ESRS framework was deliberately designed to interoperate with the ISSB's IFRS S1 and S2 standards, which India's SEBI and ICAI are actively considering for integration into the BRSR (Business Responsibility and Sustainability Reporting) framework. Companies investing in CSRD-readiness are simultaneously building the foundation for next-generation domestic compliance. The direction of travel is unambiguous: granular, assured, comparable sustainability data is becoming a non-negotiable condition of market participation globally.</p><h3>What To Do Now</h3><ul><li>Conduct a CSRD Exposure Mapping: Identify all EU-based customers, investors, and partners and assess which are already subject to CSRD Wave 1 or Wave 2. Prioritise those relationships where ESG data requests are imminent or already arriving — this is your most urgent compliance trigger.</li><li>Perform a Double Materiality Assessment (DMA): Even if not formally required under Indian law, conducting a DMA aligned with ESRS methodology will future-proof your reporting, satisfy EU value chain queries, and align with emerging BRSR Core expectations. Engage a qualified ESG advisor to facilitate the process.</li><li>Build Scope 3 Emissions Capability: ESRS E1 requires EU companies to disclose Scope 3 emissions — which means your emissions become their emissions. Begin collecting activity data across your own supply chain, establish emission factors for key materials and logistics, and invest in credible carbon accounting software or methodology.</li><li>Review and Strengthen ESG Data Governance: CSRD requires limited (and eventually reasonable) third-party assurance. Audit your current data collection processes for GHG emissions, energy, water, waste, and social indicators. Identify gaps, assign data ownership, and implement controls as if assurance is already required — because for your EU customers, it effectively is.</li><li>Engage Legal and Commercial Teams on Contractual Implications: EU companies may begin embedding CSRD data-sharing obligations into supplier contracts. Ensure your legal, procurement, and sustainability teams are aligned on what data you can provide, at what frequency, and under what confidentiality terms — before you are negotiating from a position of weakness.</li></ul><h3>Our Take</h3><p>CSRD and EFRAG's ESRS represent a structural shift, not a regulatory cycle. The EU has effectively established sustainability disclosure as a condition of market access, and through value chain provisions, that condition now applies to Indian companies whether or not they ever set foot in Brussels. What we are witnessing is the gradual emergence of a global ESG disclosure floor — shaped by CSRD in Europe, ISSB internationally, and progressively by SEBI's BRSR Core domestically. Indian companies that move early — investing in emissions accounting, double materiality assessments, and data governance — will not only avoid the commercial disruption of being unable to answer EU buyer questionnaires, but will build a genuine competitive advantage as sustainability transparency becomes a procurement and investment criterion. The question is no longer whether Indian businesses will need to report to this standard, but whether they will be ready when their largest customers demand it.</p>

item.title: Decoding ISSB Support Mechanisms: What Indian Companies Need to Know About Consistent Standards Application
item.description: The International Sustainability Standards Board (ISSB) has established a structured framework to support consistent global application of its landmark sustainability disclosure standards — IFRS S1 (General Requirements) and IFRS S2 (Climate-related Disclosures). For Indian companies navigating SEBI
item.content:encoded: <h3>Executive Summary</h3><p>The International Sustainability Standards Board (ISSB) has established a structured framework to support consistent global application of its landmark sustainability disclosure standards — IFRS S1 (General Requirements) and IFRS S2 (Climate-related Disclosures). For Indian companies navigating SEBI's Business Responsibility and Sustainability Reporting (BRSR) framework and eyeing access to global capital markets, understanding how the ISSB supports implementation is no longer optional — it is a strategic imperative. Companies that align early with ISSB guidance will be better positioned to meet converging regulatory expectations from both Indian and international regulators.</p><h3>What Changed</h3><p>The ISSB, operating under the IFRS Foundation, has published and continuously updates a suite of implementation support resources designed to help preparers, auditors, regulators, and investors apply IFRS S1 and IFRS S2 in a consistent and comparable manner across jurisdictions. These resources include educational materials, illustrative examples, frequently asked questions (FAQs), and capacity-building initiatives targeted at both developed and emerging markets. Critically, the ISSB's support framework addresses one of the most common barriers to adoption: the perception that these standards are too complex or too resource-intensive for companies outside large Western markets. The ISSB has acknowledged the varied starting points of different jurisdictions — including India — and has built transition reliefs and phased implementation pathways into its guidance. This signals a clear intention: the ISSB wants broad, global adoption, and it is actively removing friction to get there. The ISSB also works in close coordination with the International Organization of Securities Commissions (IOSCO), which has endorsed IFRS S1 and S2 for use by its 130+ member regulators — including SEBI. This regulatory endorsement chain means that ISSB standards are increasingly likely to inform future mandatory disclosure requirements in India.</p><h3>Who Is Affected</h3><ul><li>Top 1,000 listed companies in India currently subject to SEBI's mandatory BRSR Core disclosures</li><li>Companies with international listings, cross-border financing, or global investor bases seeking capital from ESG-focused funds</li><li>Indian subsidiaries and supply chain partners of multinational corporations subject to CSRD (EU) or SEC climate rules (US)</li><li>CFOs, Chief Sustainability Officers, and Investor Relations teams responsible for sustainability reporting strategy</li><li>Statutory auditors and assurance providers preparing to provide limited or reasonable assurance on sustainability disclosures</li><li>Financial institutions and banks assessing climate-related risks in their lending and investment portfolios</li><li>Mid-sized companies aspiring to join global supply chains where sustainability disclosure is becoming a procurement prerequisite</li></ul><h3>Key Obligations & Deadlines</h3><ul><li>IMMEDIATE: Familiarise your reporting team with ISSB's free implementation support portal at ifrs.org — including the IFRS S1 and S2 Basis for Conclusions documents, which explain the intent behind each requirement</li><li>IMMEDIATE: Review the ISSB's FAQ library and educational materials to identify gaps between your current BRSR disclosures and IFRS S2 climate-related disclosure requirements (especially Scope 1, 2 &amp; 3 emissions, scenario analysis, and governance disclosures)</li><li>Q1 2025 ONWARDS: Monitor SEBI communications for formal guidance on ISSB alignment — SEBI's BRSR Core framework is already conceptually aligned with TCFD (which ISSB S2 supersedes), and a formal convergence announcement is anticipated</li><li>ONGOING: Track ISSB's 'IFRS Sustainability Disclosure Taxonomy' updates, which facilitate digital, machine-readable reporting — a likely future SEBI requirement for structured data submissions</li><li>ONGOING: Engage with the ISSB's capacity-building programmes and regional workshops, particularly those conducted through the IFRS Foundation's Emerging Economies Group, in which India participates</li><li>2025–2026: Begin voluntary parallel reporting against IFRS S2 alongside mandatory BRSR submissions — this builds internal capability and signals disclosure quality to global investors</li></ul><h3>Climate Transition Angle</h3><p>The ISSB's implementation support framework is not merely a technical compliance exercise — it sits at the heart of the global architecture being built to finance the climate transition. IFRS S2 requires companies to disclose material climate-related risks and opportunities across both physical risks (extreme weather, water stress, sea-level rise) and transition risks (carbon pricing, stranded assets, changing consumer preferences). For Indian companies, both dimensions are acutely relevant: India faces some of the world's highest physical climate risk exposure, while simultaneously undertaking one of the most ambitious energy transitions of any major economy. The convergence of ISSB standards with the EU's Corporate Sustainability Reporting Directive (CSRD) — through the ongoing interoperability project between ISSB and EFRAG — means that Indian exporters and supply chain participants serving European markets will face a dual disclosure expectation. Similarly, the US SEC's climate disclosure rules, though currently under legal challenge, signal the same direction of travel. Companies that use the ISSB's support resources to build robust climate disclosure capabilities now are essentially future-proofing themselves against a cascade of regulatory requirements from multiple directions. Perhaps most significantly, consistent application of ISSB standards globally enables apples-to-apples comparison of climate risk across portfolios — which is precisely what institutional investors, green bond issuers, and development finance institutions require to allocate transition capital efficiently. Indian companies that speak the ISSB language will have a measurable advantage in accessing this growing pool of sustainability-linked finance.</p><h3>What To Do Now</h3><ul><li>CONDUCT A GAP ASSESSMENT: Map your current BRSR Core disclosures against the specific requirements of IFRS S1 and IFRS S2 — pay particular attention to climate scenario analysis, Scope 3 value chain emissions, and the 'significant judgements' disclosure requirements that most Indian companies currently omit</li><li>BUILD INTERNAL CAPABILITY: Designate a cross-functional ISSB working group (Finance, Legal, Sustainability, Operations, IR) and use the ISSB's free educational modules to upskill the team — this is foundational, not optional</li><li>ENGAGE YOUR AUDITORS EARLY: Discuss with your statutory auditor and any sustainability assurance provider how they are preparing for IFRS S2-aligned assurance engagements — the assurance landscape in India is evolving rapidly and early dialogue prevents last-minute surprises</li><li>LEVERAGE TRANSITION RELIEFS STRATEGICALLY: ISSB has built in a one-year relief from Scope 3 and some comparative reporting requirements for first-time adopters — understand these reliefs and plan your adoption roadmap to use them intelligently rather than deferring preparation</li><li>COMMUNICATE PROACTIVELY WITH INVESTORS: If you have foreign institutional investors (FIIs) or are seeking ESG-labelled debt, proactively communicate your ISSB alignment roadmap — investors increasingly distinguish between companies that are reactive to disclosure mandates and those that are building genuine climate governance</li></ul><h3>Our Take</h3><p>The ISSB's commitment to supporting consistent application of its standards is a signal that global sustainability disclosure is moving from aspiration to infrastructure. For Indian business leaders, the temptation may be to treat ISSB standards as a 'foreign' requirement relevant only to multinationals — but this view is increasingly untenable. SEBI's BRSR framework is converging with ISSB principles by design, not coincidence, and the trajectory points clearly toward mandatory ISSB-aligned disclosures for listed Indian companies within the next three to five years. The companies that will navigate this transition smoothly are those investing now in data systems, governance structures, and internal capabilities — not those scrambling to comply at the deadline. The ISSB's implementation support framework is, in effect, a public good: free, authoritative, and specifically designed to help companies like yours succeed. The question is not whether to engage with it, but how quickly.</p>

item.title: Plastic Waste Management Rules, 2016 (Amended 2018): What Every Producer, Brand Owner, and Importer Must Know
item.description: The Plastic Waste Management Rules, 2016, as amended in 2018, represent India's most comprehensive regulatory framework for reducing, managing, and recycling plastic waste — placing legal obligations squarely on producers, brand owners, importers, manufacturers, local bodies, and waste generators al
item.content:encoded: <h3>Executive Summary</h3><p>The Plastic Waste Management Rules, 2016, as amended in 2018, represent India's most comprehensive regulatory framework for reducing, managing, and recycling plastic waste — placing legal obligations squarely on producers, brand owners, importers, manufacturers, local bodies, and waste generators alike. The rules enforce the Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR) principle, mandating that those who profit from plastic products must bear the cost of managing them at end-of-life. For businesses operating in India, non-compliance is not merely a regulatory risk — it is increasingly a reputational, operational, and climate-transition risk that demands board-level attention.</p><h3>What Changed</h3><p>The Plastic Waste Management Rules, 2016, superseded the earlier Plastic Waste (Management and Handling) Rules, 2011, and were further strengthened by the 2018 amendment. The overhaul was driven by the need to move beyond compliance-on-paper toward a functioning circular economy for plastics in India. In plain terms, the rules did three significant things. First, they widened the net of who is responsible — from just manufacturers to include brand owners, importers, producers, local bodies, Gram Panchayats, and institutional waste generators such as hospitals, hotels, malls, schools, and government offices. Second, they embedded the 'polluter pays' principle by formalising Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR), making producers legally accountable for the environmentally sound management of their plastic products through their entire lifecycle — including post-consumer waste collection and recycling. Third, they introduced clearer definitions for critical terms — including multi-layered packaging (MLP), compostable plastics, energy recovery, and alternate use — to close loopholes and ensure that a wider range of plastic materials falls under regulatory oversight. The 2018 amendment further tightened these provisions, particularly around registration, EPR implementation timelines, and the inclusion of street vendors and waste pickers as recognised participants in the waste management value chain — a progressive move that formalises the role of India's informal recycling economy.</p><h3>Who Is Affected</h3><ul><li>Producers: Any entity manufacturing or importing carry bags, plastic sheets, multi-layered packaging (MLP), or similar plastic products for sale or use in India.</li><li>Brand Owners: Companies selling any commodity under a registered brand label that uses plastic packaging — this includes FMCG, food &amp; beverage, pharmaceuticals, e-commerce, and consumer durables sectors.</li><li>Importers: Any entity holding an Importer-Exporter Code (IEC) that brings plastic products or plastic-packaged goods into India.</li><li>Manufacturers of Plastic Raw Materials: Entities producing plastic polymers and resins used as raw materials by downstream producers.</li><li>Institutional Waste Generators: Occupiers of offices (government and private), hospitals, schools, colleges, hotels, restaurants, malls, and shopping complexes.</li><li>Local Bodies &amp; Gram Panchayats: Municipal corporations, nagar panchayats, and village-level bodies responsible for waste collection, segregation, and processing infrastructure.</li><li>Waste Generators (General): Households, commercial establishments, and industrial units that generate plastic waste.</li><li>Note: Export-Oriented Units (EOUs) and Special Economic Zone (SEZ) units are partially exempt from Rule 4 — but this exemption does NOT apply to units packaging gutkha, tobacco, or pan masala, nor to surplus or rejected products.</li></ul><h3>Key Obligations & Deadlines</h3><ul><li>EPR Registration: Producers, brand owners, and importers must register with the State Pollution Control Board (SPCB) or Pollution Control Committee (PCC) to obtain consent to establish and operate facilities handling plastic waste.</li><li>Minimum Thickness Compliance: Carry bags must meet prescribed minimum thickness standards (as notified separately by MoEFCC/CPCB) to ensure recyclability — non-compliant carry bags are prohibited from manufacture, import, stocking, distribution, sale, and use.</li><li>Source Segregation: Waste generators — including institutional generators such as offices, hotels, hospitals, and malls — are obligated to segregate plastic waste at the point of generation and hand it over to authorised waste collectors or recyclers.</li><li>EPR Plan Implementation: Producers, brand owners, and importers must establish systems for collection, channelisation, and recycling of post-consumer plastic waste, in accordance with EPR guidelines issued by CPCB. This includes setting up or partnering with Producer Responsibility Organisations (PROs).</li><li>Multi-Layered Packaging (MLP) Phase-Out: MLPs that are non-recyclable, non-energy-recoverable, or have no alternate use must be phased out. Producers using MLP must establish a collection-back system for used packaging.</li><li>Local Body Obligations: Urban local bodies and Gram Panchayats must establish systems and infrastructure for collection, segregation, storage, transportation, processing, and disposal of plastic waste within their jurisdictions.</li><li>Plastic Waste Processors: Recyclers, co-processors (cement kilns, waste-to-energy), and processors must register with the SPCB/PCC and operate only in accordance with prescribed environmental conditions.</li><li>Labelling Requirements: Producers of plastic carry bags and multi-layered packaging must ensure products carry prescribed labels indicating material composition, recyclability status, and manufacturer details.</li><li>Street Vendor Compliance: Street vendors using plastic carry bags must comply with prescribed standards on thickness and type of plastic used.</li><li>Ongoing Reporting: Producers, importers, and brand owners must maintain records of plastic raw material used and plastic waste managed, and submit annual returns to the prescribed authority.</li></ul><h3>Climate Transition Angle</h3><p>Plastic waste management sits at the intersection of two of the most urgent dimensions of the climate crisis: the carbon footprint of virgin plastic production and the physical risk of plastic pollution to ecosystems that underpin economic activity. Globally, plastics are responsible for approximately 3.4% of annual greenhouse gas emissions — the majority of which come from the production of virgin fossil-fuel-based polymers. Every tonne of plastic that is collected and recycled rather than landfilled or incinerated represents a direct reduction in the demand for virgin plastic and, therefore, in Scope 3 emissions for brand owners and retailers. For Indian businesses, this has immediate relevance to global supply chain compliance. The European Union's Corporate Sustainability Reporting Directive (CSRD) and the ISSB's IFRS S2 climate disclosure standards are accelerating requirements for multinational buyers to assess and report on the plastic and packaging footprint of their entire value chain — including Indian suppliers and contract manufacturers. Indian exporters who cannot demonstrate compliant plastic waste management practices, EPR registration, and measurable recycled content in their packaging risk losing access to European and North American markets. Furthermore, physical climate risks — including flooding amplified by choked waterways and drainage systems due to plastic waste — directly threaten industrial facilities, logistics infrastructure, and agricultural supply chains across India. The CPCB's own data consistently shows that uncollected plastic waste is a primary contributor to urban flooding in cities like Mumbai, Chennai, and Kolkata. For real estate, insurance, and infrastructure sectors, this is already a material financial risk. Robust compliance with the Plastic Waste Management Rules is therefore not merely a legal obligation — it is a forward-looking risk management strategy aligned with India's NDC commitments and the global net-zero transition.</p><h3>What To Do Now</h3><ul><li>Conduct a Plastic Footprint Audit: Map all plastic materials used across your products, packaging, and operations — including carry bags, MLP, plastic sheets, and single-use plastics. Quantify volumes by material type and assess current recycled content versus virgin plastic use. This audit forms the baseline for EPR compliance planning and voluntary sustainability disclosures under frameworks such as BRSR (Business Responsibility and Sustainability Reporting) and ISSB S2.</li><li>Register Under EPR Immediately if Not Already Done: Producers, brand owners, and importers who have not yet registered on the CPCB's centralised EPR portal (https://eprnp.cpcb.gov.in) should do so without delay. Failure to register is a direct violation of the Rules and exposes companies to enforcement action by SPCBs, including closure notices and penalties under the Environment (Protection) Act, 1986.</li><li>Establish or Join a Producer Responsibility Organisation (PRO): Rather than building an individual plastic waste collection system from scratch, evaluate partnerships with CPCB-registered PROs or industry-level plastic waste management consortia. PROs provide economies of scale for collection, aggregation, and recycling — particularly critical for mid-sized producers and brand owners who lack the operational infrastructure to manage EPR obligations independently.</li><li>Phase Out Non-Recyclable Multi-Layered Packaging: Begin a time-bound packaging redesign process to eliminate MLPs that have no recycling or energy recovery pathway. Engage packaging suppliers and R&amp;D teams to identify mono-material or easily recyclable alternatives. Align this with global packaging sustainability commitments (e.g., Ellen MacArthur Foundation's Global Commitment) to strengthen ESG positioning with international investors and buyers.</li><li>Train Compliance and Operations Teams: Ensure that procurement, operations, and facility management teams — particularly at institutional waste generator sites such as offices, factories, warehouses, hotels, and canteens — are trained on source segregation obligations, authorised waste handover procedures, and record-keeping requirements. Designate a Plastic Waste Management Officer responsible for internal compliance monitoring and annual return filings with the SPCB.</li></ul><h3>Our Take</h3><p>The Plastic Waste Management Rules, 2016 (as amended 2018) were a landmark step — but enforcement has historically been uneven, and many producers and brand owners have treated EPR as a checkbox exercise rather than a genuine operational commitment. That era is ending. CPCB and SPCBs have significantly stepped up scrutiny, the EPR portal has made non-compliance easier to identify, and global trade partners are embedding plastic accountability into procurement contracts and supplier codes of conduct. More importantly, the MoEFCC's subsequent notifications — including the Plastic Waste Management (Amendment) Rules, 2021 and 2022 — have dramatically expanded the scope of banned single-use plastics and raised EPR targets, building directly on the 2016/2018 framework. Companies that have not yet internalised these rules into their core business strategy — rather than treating them as a purely legal or government-affairs matter — are accumulating both regulatory and reputational liability. The direction of travel is unambiguous: India is tightening its plastic governance regime in lockstep with global standards, and businesses that move early on EPR compliance, packaging redesign, and plastic footprint disclosure will hold a meaningful competitive advantage in both domestic and international markets in the years ahead.</p>

item.title: GHG Protocol Standards & Guidance: The Accounting Backbone Every Indian Company Needs to Understand
item.description: The GHG Protocol provides the world's most widely used greenhouse gas accounting and reporting standards, forming the methodological foundation for virtually every major climate disclosure framework — including SEBI's BRSR Core, the ISSB's IFRS S2, and the EU's CSRD. Indian companies that fail to gr
item.content:encoded: <h3>Executive Summary</h3><p>The GHG Protocol provides the world's most widely used greenhouse gas accounting and reporting standards, forming the methodological foundation for virtually every major climate disclosure framework — including SEBI's BRSR Core, the ISSB's IFRS S2, and the EU's CSRD. Indian companies that fail to ground their emissions reporting in GHG Protocol methodology risk producing disclosures that are inconsistent, non-comparable, and increasingly unacceptable to regulators, investors, and global supply chain partners. Understanding and correctly applying these standards is no longer optional — it is the baseline for credible climate action.</p><h3>What Changed</h3><p>The GHG Protocol, jointly developed by the World Resources Institute (WRI) and the World Business Council for Sustainable Development (WBCSD), is not a single regulation but a suite of standards and guidance documents that define how organisations measure, manage, and report their greenhouse gas emissions. The core suite includes the Corporate Accounting and Reporting Standard (the foundational standard for Scope 1 and 2 emissions), the Scope 3 Standard (covering value chain emissions across 15 categories), the Market-Based Accounting Guidance (for Scope 2 electricity reporting), and the Land Sector and Removals Guidance, among others. While the GHG Protocol itself is not a regulatory mandate in India, it is the methodological engine that powers frameworks that are mandated — most notably SEBI's Business Responsibility and Sustainability Reporting (BRSR) framework, which explicitly references GHG Protocol definitions for Scope 1, Scope 2, and Scope 3 emissions. As India's regulatory expectations on climate disclosure intensify, and as global standard-setters converge on GHG Protocol methodology, fluency in these standards has become a core corporate competency. Critically, the GHG Protocol is currently undergoing its most significant update in over a decade. WRI and WBCSD have launched a formal review process to update the Corporate Standard and the Scope 3 Standard, with stakeholder consultations ongoing. The updated standards are expected to reflect advances in climate science, greater alignment with ISSB and TCFD frameworks, and stronger guidance on avoided emissions, removals, and net-zero target-setting. Indian companies should monitor these developments closely, as updated standards will cascade into BRSR and other domestic requirements.</p><h3>Who Is Affected</h3><ul><li>Top 1000 listed companies required to submit BRSR reports under SEBI's mandate — GHG Protocol methodology underpins all emissions disclosures</li><li>Companies in the BRSR Core cohort (initially the top 150 by market capitalisation) subject to reasonable assurance on ESG disclosures, where methodological rigour is directly scrutinised</li><li>Export-oriented manufacturers and suppliers in sectors such as textiles, chemicals, steel, cement, and automotive whose global buyers require GHG Protocol-aligned Scope 3 data</li><li>Companies subject to the EU Carbon Border Adjustment Mechanism (CBAM) — iron, steel, cement, aluminium, fertilisers, electricity, and hydrogen exporters to the EU</li><li>Indian subsidiaries of multinational corporations whose parent companies report under CSRD, CDP, or ISSB frameworks</li><li>Companies pursuing Science Based Targets (SBTi) validation, which mandates GHG Protocol-compliant emissions inventories</li><li>Financial institutions and asset managers conducting portfolio emissions assessments under PCAF (Partnership for Carbon Accounting Financials), which is built on GHG Protocol principles</li></ul><h3>Key Obligations & Deadlines</h3><ul><li>BRSR Core Compliance (FY 2023-24 onwards): Top 150 listed companies must disclose Scope 1, Scope 2, and material Scope 3 emissions using GHG Protocol-aligned methodology, subject to reasonable assurance by a third-party auditor</li><li>BRSR Extended Rollout: SEBI is progressively expanding BRSR Core requirements to the top 1000 listed companies — companies outside the initial cohort should treat FY 2024-25 as a preparation year to build GHG accounting capability</li><li>EU CBAM Transition Period (now live): Indian exporters of in-scope goods to the EU must report embedded emissions using methodologies consistent with GHG Protocol principles; full financial obligations under CBAM commence in 2026</li><li>CDP Disclosure Cycle (annual, typically April–July submission window): CDP questionnaires for Climate, Water, and Forests require GHG Protocol-aligned data; scores directly influence investor and customer assessments</li><li>GHG Protocol Corporate Standard Update (expected 2025-2026): Monitor WRI/WBCSD stakeholder consultation outputs and prepare to transition to updated standards once finalised — establish a methodology review process now</li><li>Science Based Targets (SBTi) Validation: Companies committing to net-zero targets must submit GHG Protocol-compliant base-year inventories; SBTi has tightened validation timelines and criteria in its Corporate Net-Zero Standard</li><li>Ongoing: Establish or review organisational and operational boundaries, consolidation approaches (equity share vs. operational control vs. financial control), and Scope 3 materiality assessments in line with GHG Protocol guidance</li></ul><h3>Climate Transition Angle</h3><p>The GHG Protocol is not merely an accounting exercise — it is the infrastructure through which physical climate risks and transition risks become quantifiable, comparable, and actionable. Without a robust GHG inventory, companies cannot credibly assess their exposure to carbon pricing, stranded asset risk, or the carbon intensity demands of their customers and capital providers. From a supply chain perspective, Scope 3 accounting — the most complex and consequential part of the GHG Protocol suite — is rapidly becoming a competitive differentiator and a commercial requirement. Global buyers, particularly in Europe and North America, are embedding Scope 3 data requests into procurement contracts. Indian suppliers that cannot provide verified, GHG Protocol-aligned emissions data risk losing market access, regardless of the quality of their products. The convergence of global disclosure frameworks around GHG Protocol methodology is accelerating. IFRS S2 (the ISSB's climate disclosure standard) explicitly references GHG Protocol for emissions measurement. The EU's CSRD and its underlying European Sustainability Reporting Standards (ESRS) do the same. The SEC's climate disclosure rule in the United States, though facing legal challenges, also draws on GHG Protocol concepts. For Indian companies with cross-border operations, listings, or investor bases, GHG Protocol fluency is the common language of global climate finance and trade. Looking ahead, the ongoing update to the GHG Protocol Corporate Standard is expected to introduce stronger guidance on the treatment of carbon removals, the distinction between gross and net emissions, and the integration of physical risk data into transition planning. Companies that build deep internal capability in GHG accounting now will be far better positioned to adapt to these evolving requirements — and to use their emissions data strategically for target-setting, capital allocation, and stakeholder communication.</p><h3>What To Do Now</h3><ul><li>Conduct a GHG Protocol Methodology Audit: Review your existing Scope 1, 2, and 3 inventory against the current GHG Protocol Corporate Standard. Identify gaps in boundary-setting, emission factor selection, data quality, and documentation — particularly if your current disclosures were prepared without specialist input</li><li>Map Scope 3 Materiality: Use the GHG Protocol Scope 3 Standard's screening methodology to identify which of the 15 categories are material to your business. Prioritise upstream categories (purchased goods and services, capital goods, fuel and energy) and downstream categories (use of sold products, end-of-life treatment) based on sector relevance and data availability</li><li>Establish an Emissions Data Management System: Move beyond spreadsheet-based tracking. Invest in a dedicated GHG accounting platform or ERP-integrated solution that supports audit trails, emission factor libraries, uncertainty assessments, and scenario modelling — all requirements for third-party assurance under BRSR Core</li><li>Monitor the GHG Protocol Update Process: Assign a member of your sustainability team to track WRI/WBCSD consultation documents and draft standard releases. Participate in stakeholder consultations where possible. Begin assessing how proposed changes — particularly on Scope 3 recalculation triggers and removals accounting — may affect your reported figures</li><li>Align Internal Capacity with External Assurance Requirements: Train finance, procurement, operations, and sustainability teams on GHG Protocol fundamentals. Engage your statutory auditor or a specialist ESG assurance provider early to understand what evidence and controls they will require for BRSR Core reasonable assurance — and build those systems before the reporting deadline</li></ul><h3>Our Take</h3><p>The GHG Protocol is the closest thing the climate disclosure world has to a universal accounting standard, and its influence on Indian corporate reporting is only going to deepen. As SEBI tightens BRSR requirements, as CBAM reshapes the economics of exports to Europe, and as institutional investors apply increasingly sophisticated climate lenses to portfolio analysis, the quality of a company's GHG accounting will be read as a proxy for the quality of its overall climate governance. Companies that treat GHG Protocol compliance as a checkbox exercise — producing numbers without understanding the methodology, boundaries, or uncertainty behind them — will find themselves exposed when assurance providers, regulators, or activist investors probe the detail. The upcoming update to the Corporate Standard is a pivotal moment: it will likely raise the bar on Scope 3 rigour and bring GHG accounting closer to financial accounting in terms of auditability and precision. Indian business leaders who invest now in building genuine internal capability — not just in reporting, but in using emissions data to drive strategic decisions — will be the ones setting the terms of the climate transition, rather than scrambling to keep up with it.</p>

item.title: SEBI's ESG Consultation Paper: What India's Capital Markets Are Signalling About the Future of Sustainable Finance
item.description: In February 2023, SEBI released a landmark Consultation Paper on ESG Disclosures, Ratings and Investing, signalling a comprehensive effort to strengthen the architecture of sustainable finance in India's capital markets. The paper proposes reforms across three interconnected pillars — corporate ESG
item.content:encoded: <h3>Executive Summary</h3><p>In February 2023, SEBI released a landmark Consultation Paper on ESG Disclosures, Ratings and Investing, signalling a comprehensive effort to strengthen the architecture of sustainable finance in India's capital markets. The paper proposes reforms across three interconnected pillars — corporate ESG disclosures under BRSR, the regulation of ESG Rating Providers (ERPs), and standards for ESG-themed investment products such as mutual funds. For listed companies, institutional investors, and rating agencies, this consultation marks the beginning of a more rigorous, accountable, and globally comparable ESG regime in India.</p><h3>What Changed</h3><p>SEBI's February 2023 Consultation Paper does not itself enact new rules — rather, it invites public comment on a suite of proposed reforms that are expected to translate into binding regulations. This is an important distinction: companies and investors should treat it as a preview of the regulatory direction, not merely an academic exercise. The paper addresses three major areas. First, it proposes enhancements to the Business Responsibility and Sustainability Report (BRSR) framework, including the introduction of a 'BRSR Core' — a shorter, high-priority set of Key Performance Indicators (KPIs) subject to reasonable assurance by third parties. This would make a subset of ESG disclosures verifiable and auditable, raising the bar significantly from the current self-reported model. Second, the paper proposes a regulatory framework for ESG Rating Providers (ERPs) operating in India. Currently, ESG ratings are largely unregulated, creating risks of inconsistency, conflicts of interest, and opacity. SEBI proposes that ERPs be registered, follow defined methodologies, and ensure transparency in how ratings are derived — a move that mirrors regulatory trends in the EU and the UK. Third, the paper scrutinises ESG-themed mutual funds and other investment products, proposing guardrails to prevent greenwashing — the practice of marketing financial products as sustainable without substantive underlying criteria. This includes minimum thresholds for ESG-aligned investments within such funds and stronger disclosure obligations for asset managers.</p><h3>Who Is Affected</h3><ul><li>Top 1,000 listed companies (by market capitalisation) — already subject to BRSR; the proposed 'BRSR Core' with assured KPIs will directly impact their disclosure and audit processes</li><li>All listed companies — broader BRSR enhancements may progressively extend to a wider universe of listed entities</li><li>ESG Rating Providers (ERPs) — domestic and foreign agencies providing ESG ratings on Indian companies will face mandatory registration and conduct requirements</li><li>Asset Management Companies (AMCs) — mutual funds offering ESG-labelled schemes will need to meet new portfolio composition, disclosure, and anti-greenwashing standards</li><li>Foreign Portfolio Investors (FPIs) — those investing in Indian markets may face new ESG-related disclosure or engagement obligations</li><li>Sustainability consultants and ESG assurance providers — demand for third-party assurance of BRSR Core KPIs will create both opportunity and responsibility for assurance professionals</li></ul><h3>Key Obligations & Deadlines</h3><ul><li>BRSR Core KPIs: SEBI proposed a set of ~50 high-priority ESG indicators spanning environment, social, and governance themes, with mandatory reasonable assurance — companies should begin gap assessments against these indicators immediately</li><li>Third-Party Assurance: The 'BRSR Core' framework proposes that disclosures be subject to third-party reasonable assurance, likely phased in starting with the top 150 listed companies before expanding further — identify and onboard qualified assurance providers now</li><li>ESG Rating Provider Registration: ERPs will be required to register with SEBI, disclose their methodologies, manage conflicts of interest, and publish ratings with explanatory rationale — ERPs should prepare compliance frameworks ahead of formal rule notification</li><li>ESG Mutual Fund Guardrails: AMCs offering ESG schemes must ensure a minimum proportion of assets are invested in companies meeting defined ESG criteria, with enhanced scheme-level disclosures — AMCs should review existing fund mandates for alignment</li><li>Consultation Response Deadline: The public comment period for this consultation paper closed in early 2023; formal regulatory circulars translating these proposals into rules are expected to follow — monitor SEBI notifications closely for binding implementation dates</li><li>Value Chain Disclosures: SEBI proposed that large listed companies disclose ESG performance of their top suppliers and customers (Scope 3 equivalent) — companies should begin supplier ESG data collection programmes</li></ul><h3>Climate Transition Angle</h3><p>SEBI's consultation paper is best understood not in isolation, but as India's contribution to a global convergence of sustainability disclosure standards. The proposed BRSR Core KPIs include climate-specific metrics — greenhouse gas emissions (Scope 1, 2, and progressively Scope 3), energy consumption, water intensity, and waste generation — that closely mirror the climate-related disclosure requirements under the ISSB's IFRS S2 standard and the EU's Corporate Sustainability Reporting Directive (CSRD). Indian companies with international operations, foreign investors, or global supply chain relationships are increasingly expected to report in a manner consistent with these frameworks. The push for assured disclosures is particularly significant from a climate transition perspective. Investors and lenders globally are using ESG data to make capital allocation decisions aligned with net-zero pathways. Unverified, inconsistent data undermines this process. By introducing assurance requirements, SEBI is laying the groundwork for Indian capital markets to support credible climate transition finance — including green bonds, sustainability-linked loans, and transition finance instruments. The value chain disclosure proposals also carry material implications for physical and transition climate risks. Companies that map their supplier ESG performance will, by extension, begin to understand where climate-related vulnerabilities sit in their supply chains — whether that is water-stressed agricultural suppliers, carbon-intensive raw material producers, or geographically exposed logistics partners. This is precisely the kind of systemic risk visibility that regulators, investors, and boards need as India's climate transition accelerates.</p><h3>What To Do Now</h3><ul><li>Conduct a BRSR Core Gap Assessment: Map your current disclosures against the proposed ~50 BRSR Core KPIs. Identify data gaps, especially for GHG emissions (Scope 1 &amp; 2 as a minimum), water withdrawal, and employee safety metrics — these are most likely to be prioritised in the final rules</li><li>Appoint an Assurance Provider Early: The market for BRSR assurance services is nascent and will become competitive quickly. Engage with a qualified assurance provider (typically a Big 4 or specialist sustainability assurance firm) to understand the readiness requirements and timeline for reasonable assurance</li><li>Build Supplier ESG Data Infrastructure: Begin collecting basic ESG data from your top 20–30 suppliers in preparation for value chain disclosure obligations. A simple supplier ESG questionnaire aligned to BRSR metrics is a practical starting point</li><li>Review ESG-Labelled Financial Products: If you are an AMC or advise on ESG fund structuring, conduct a portfolio review against SEBI's proposed minimum ESG investment thresholds and update fund disclosures to pre-empt greenwashing scrutiny</li><li>Monitor SEBI Notifications and Engage Proactively: Track SEBI's regulatory calendar for formal circulars following this consultation. Consider engaging through industry associations (CII, FICCI, AMFI) to provide feedback that shapes implementation timelines and definitions favourably for your sector</li></ul><h3>Our Take</h3><p>SEBI's February 2023 Consultation Paper is one of the most significant regulatory signals in India's ESG history — not because it changes rules today, but because it maps out a coherent, ambitious architecture for sustainable finance that will define the next decade of corporate disclosure and investment practice. The introduction of assured BRSR Core disclosures is a genuine step-change: it moves India from a disclosure culture based on self-reporting and box-ticking toward one grounded in accountability and comparability. The regulation of ESG Rating Providers addresses a long-standing market integrity gap that has undermined investor confidence in ESG data quality. What comes next will matter enormously — the specificity of the final KPI definitions, the pace of the assurance roll-out, and the stringency of anti-greenwashing rules for funds will determine whether India's ESG market matures into a credible force for capital reallocation, or remains a compliance exercise. Companies that start building data systems, assurance relationships, and supply chain visibility now will not only be compliant — they will be competitively positioned as global investors increasingly scrutinise India's listed universe through a sustainability lens.</p>

item.title: CPCB Directs Customs Release for 106 EEE Items: What Importers and EPR-Registered Producers Must Know
item.description: On 31 August 2023, the Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB) issued a formal letter to Customs Authorities directing the release of imported consignments belonging to producers of 106 specified Electrical and Electronic Equipment (EEE) items. This intervention signals that CPCB is actively coordina
