item.content:encoded: <h3>Executive Summary</h3><p>On 31 August 2023, the Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB) issued a formal letter to Customs Authorities directing the release of imported consignments belonging to producers of 106 specified Electrical and Electronic Equipment (EEE) items. This intervention signals that CPCB is actively coordinating with border agencies to enforce EPR compliance as a pre-condition for market access. Companies importing or manufacturing EEE products must treat valid EPR registration — and timely target fulfilment — as a non-negotiable gateway to trade, not merely a back-office reporting obligation.</p><h3>What Changed</h3><p>Under India's E-Waste (Management) Rules, 2022, producers, importers, and brand owners of Electrical and Electronic Equipment are required to register on the CPCB EPR portal and meet annual e-waste collection and channelisation targets. When a producer fails to demonstrate valid EPR registration or compliance status, Customs Authorities can — and increasingly do — hold consignments at the port of entry. The CPCB letter dated 31 August 2023 represents a specific directive issued on behalf of 106 EEE producers, instructing Customs to release their consignments. This type of letter is typically issued when a producer can demonstrate active EPR registration and/or partial or full target fulfilment, resolving a hold that had been placed on their imports. The significance lies in the precedent: CPCB is functioning as an active gatekeeper between the EPR registry and physical trade flows, meaning lapses in EPR compliance can now directly disrupt supply chains at the border.</p><h3>Who Is Affected</h3><ul><li>Producers and importers of any of the 106 EEE categories listed under Schedule I of the E-Waste (Management) Rules, 2022 — including IT and telecom equipment, consumer electronics, large and small household appliances, lighting equipment, and solar panels.</li><li>Indian subsidiaries and branches of multinational electronics brands that import finished goods or semi-finished assemblies into India.</li><li>Original Equipment Manufacturers (OEMs) sourcing components internationally, where the importing entity qualifies as a 'producer' under the Rules.</li><li>E-commerce platforms and marketplace sellers who qualify as producers under the Rules by virtue of selling EEE products under their own brand or through private labels.</li><li>Logistics and customs clearance agents acting on behalf of EEE producers — they must be aware of EPR compliance status before filing Bills of Entry.</li><li>Compliance and legal teams at mid-to-large electronics companies who may not have connected their customs clearance workflows to EPR registration status.</li></ul><h3>Key Obligations & Deadlines</h3><ul><li>EPR Registration (Immediate / Ongoing): All producers and importers of EEE items must hold a valid, active EPR registration on the CPCB centralised portal (https://eprewaste.cpcb.gov.in) before importing any consignment. Registration must be renewed and kept current.</li><li>Annual EPR Targets (Financial Year Basis): Producers must meet e-waste collection targets as specified in their EPR plan. Targets are calculated as a percentage of the quantity of EEE placed on the market in prior years — non-fulfilment triggers hold notices at Customs.</li><li>EPR Certificate Submission to Customs: At the time of import, producers or their agents should be prepared to furnish EPR registration certificates and compliance status documents to Customs Authorities as proof of adherence.</li><li>Extended Producer Responsibility Plan Filing: Producers must file an annual EPR plan with CPCB at the start of each financial year, detailing projected sales volumes and corresponding collection commitments.</li><li>Consignment Release Letters (As Needed): If a consignment is held, producers must proactively engage CPCB with evidence of registration and target compliance to obtain a release letter — a process that can take days to weeks, causing costly demurrage and supply chain disruption.</li><li>Quarterly / Annual Reporting: EPR-registered producers must submit periodic reports on e-waste collected, channelised to authorised dismantlers/recyclers, and certificates obtained — failures here can trigger future holds.</li><li>Deadline Reference — 31 August 2023: The CPCB letter was issued on this date for 106 specific producers. Companies not covered by this letter should treat this as a warning signal and self-audit their EPR status immediately.</li></ul><h3>Climate Transition Angle</h3><p>E-waste is one of the fastest-growing waste streams globally, and its proper management sits at the intersection of the circular economy, toxic material control, and climate-related physical risk. Electronic devices contain critical minerals — lithium, cobalt, rare earth elements — whose extraction carries significant carbon and biodiversity costs. By enforcing EPR at the import stage, India is embedding circular economy principles directly into trade infrastructure, ensuring that the end-of-life cost of electronics is internalised by those who profit from their sale. From a global standards perspective, the International Sustainability Standards Board (ISSB) under IFRS S2 requires companies to disclose material climate-related risks across their value chains, including those arising from regulatory non-compliance. A supply chain disruption caused by a Customs hold due to EPR non-compliance is precisely the kind of transition risk — regulatory and operational — that investors and lenders are beginning to scrutinise. Similarly, the EU's Corporate Sustainability Reporting Directive (CSRD) and its underlying European Sustainability Reporting Standards (ESRS) require companies with EU market exposure to report on circular economy metrics, including e-waste management practices in global operations. For Indian EEE producers with export ambitions or foreign investment, demonstrating robust domestic EPR compliance is increasingly a credential, not just a legal obligation. It signals governance maturity, supply chain resilience, and readiness for the global regulatory environment that is rapidly converging on extended producer responsibility as a baseline requirement.</p><h3>What To Do Now</h3><ul><li>Conduct an immediate EPR compliance audit: Verify that your company's EPR registration on the CPCB portal is active, reflects current product categories and sales volumes, and that your EPR plan for FY 2023-24 has been filed. If you have not yet registered, initiate the process without delay — Customs holds are operational, not theoretical.</li><li>Integrate EPR status into customs clearance workflows: Brief your customs agents, freight forwarders, and import-export teams that EPR registration certificates must be validated before filing Bills of Entry for EEE goods. Create a pre-shipment checklist that includes EPR compliance as a mandatory clearance criterion.</li><li>Verify and document e-waste target fulfilment: Confirm with your authorised e-waste dismantlers and recyclers that collection certificates (EPR certificates) have been issued and uploaded to the CPCB portal for all quantities claimed. Gaps here are the most common trigger for Customs holds.</li><li>Establish a CPCB liaison protocol: Designate a senior compliance officer or engage a regulatory consultant with direct CPCB contact experience. In the event of a Customs hold, the ability to respond within 24-48 hours with the correct documentation can mean the difference between a minor delay and weeks of costly demurrage.</li><li>Map your product portfolio against the 106 EEE categories: Ensure that every SKU you import or manufacture is correctly categorised under the E-Waste Rules schedules and that corresponding EPR targets are reflected in your plan. Miscategorisation is a common compliance gap that surfaces only at the point of enforcement.</li></ul><h3>Our Take</h3><p>The CPCB's August 2023 letter to Customs Authorities is a small but telling document. It confirms what many compliance teams have been slow to accept: EPR in India has moved beyond a registration exercise into an enforcement regime with real teeth at the border. The fact that 106 producers needed a release letter on a single date suggests both that Customs holds are being applied at scale and that CPCB is managing a high volume of compliance-linked trade interventions. Looking ahead, we expect this coordination between CPCB and Customs to deepen — potentially including automated data-sharing between the EPR portal and the ICEGATE customs system, which would make real-time compliance status checks at the point of import a reality. Companies that treat EPR as a strategic compliance priority today — rather than a paperwork afterthought — will have a material operational advantage as enforcement intensifies. For sustainability and ESG officers, this is also a disclosure moment: supply chain disruptions caused by regulatory non-compliance in environmental obligations are precisely the kind of governance failures that institutional investors are flagging in ESG due diligence. Get ahead of it now.</p>

item.title: SBTi Services Explained: What Indian Companies Need to Know About Science-Based Targets
item.description: The Science Based Targets initiative (SBTi) provides the globally recognized framework for companies to set credible, Paris Agreement-aligned emissions reduction targets — and demand from Indian corporates, investors, and supply chain partners to adopt SBTi commitments is accelerating rapidly. As ES
item.content:encoded: <h3>Executive Summary</h3><p>The Science Based Targets initiative (SBTi) provides the globally recognized framework for companies to set credible, Paris Agreement-aligned emissions reduction targets — and demand from Indian corporates, investors, and supply chain partners to adopt SBTi commitments is accelerating rapidly. As ESG disclosure requirements tighten under SEBI's BRSR Core framework and global customers apply Scope 3 pressure across supply chains, obtaining SBTi validation is shifting from a voluntary differentiator to a commercial and regulatory necessity. Indian companies that act now will be better positioned to access green finance, retain global customers, and meet emerging mandatory climate disclosure standards.</p><h3>What Changed</h3><p>The Science Based Targets initiative (SBTi) is a partnership between CDP, the United Nations Global Compact (UNGC), World Resources Institute (WRI), and the World Wide Fund for Nature (WWF). It defines and promotes best practices in science-based target setting, independently assesses and approves companies' targets, and is widely regarded as the gold standard for corporate climate commitments globally. SBTi offers a suite of services and standards that allow companies to commit to reducing greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions in line with climate science — specifically, pathways consistent with limiting global warming to 1.5°C above pre-industrial levels as required by the Paris Agreement. The SBTi Corporate Standard covers Scope 1 (direct emissions), Scope 2 (purchased energy), and increasingly Scope 3 (value chain) emissions, requiring companies to set near-term targets (5–10 years) and long-term net-zero targets (by 2050 at the latest). Recent updates to the SBTi framework include the release of the Net-Zero Standard, the FLAG (Forest, Land and Agriculture) Guidance for land-intensive sectors, and ongoing development of sector-specific pathways for steel, cement, chemicals, apparel, and financial institutions — all of which are highly relevant to India's industrial base. The SBTi also updated its Corporate Manual in 2023–2024, tightening criteria around Scope 3 coverage thresholds and the use of carbon credits, signaling a continued move toward greater rigor and transparency.</p><h3>Who Is Affected</h3><ul><li>Large Indian exporters supplying to European, US, and UK markets where customers face CSRD or SEC climate disclosure obligations and are pushing Scope 3 targets down their supply chains</li><li>Listed companies subject to SEBI's BRSR Core mandatory ESG disclosures, particularly the top 150 listed entities by market capitalization from FY2024–25</li><li>Manufacturing, steel, cement, textiles, chemicals, and agribusiness companies covered by SBTi's sector-specific standards including FLAG guidance</li><li>Financial institutions — banks, NBFCs, and asset managers — as the SBTi Financial Institutions (SBTI-FI) standard requires financed emissions targets and portfolio alignment</li><li>Indian subsidiaries of multinational corporations whose parent companies have existing SBTi commitments and require value chain alignment</li><li>SMEs increasingly being asked by large buyers, investors, or global reporting frameworks to demonstrate emissions reduction commitments consistent with science-based pathways</li><li>Companies seeking green bonds, sustainability-linked loans, or ESG-indexed investment, where SBTi validation provides credibility to climate claims</li></ul><h3>Key Obligations & Deadlines</h3><ul><li>Commitment Letter: Companies must submit a commitment letter to SBTi pledging to set science-based targets; once submitted, targets must be developed and submitted for validation within 24 months</li><li>Near-Term Targets: Companies must set targets covering at least 67% of Scope 1 and 2 emissions, and if Scope 3 exceeds 40% of total emissions, must set a Scope 3 target — targets must show emissions reductions consistent with a 1.5°C pathway</li><li>Net-Zero Target (SBTi Net-Zero Standard): Companies committing under the Net-Zero Standard must set both a near-term and a long-term net-zero target, with net-zero no later than 2050; residual emissions must be neutralized through permanent carbon removal</li><li>Annual Disclosure: Validated companies must disclose progress against their SBTi targets annually through CDP or equivalent public reporting platforms — non-disclosure can lead to removal from the SBTi's public Companies Taking Action list</li><li>Scope 3 Coverage Threshold: From 2024 onward, SBTi requires companies in most sectors to address Scope 3 emissions if they constitute more than 40% of total GHG footprint — most Indian manufacturers and retailers will exceed this threshold</li><li>FLAG Sector Deadline: Companies in forest, land, and agriculture sectors (including food &amp; beverage, paper, palm oil) must align with FLAG guidance — SBTi has indicated these companies must include land-sector targets as part of their validated commitments</li><li>Financial Institutions: Banks and asset managers must set portfolio-level financed emissions targets under the SBTi-FI framework, covering lending and investment portfolios aligned with sectoral decarbonization approaches (SDA)</li><li>Carbon Credit Restriction: As of updated SBTi guidance, carbon offsets cannot be used to achieve near-term science-based targets — only genuine in-house or value chain emissions reductions count toward SBTi compliance</li></ul><h3>Climate Transition Angle</h3><p>SBTi sits at the intersection of physical climate risk management and transition risk strategy. Companies that set science-based targets are, by definition, building a roadmap to decarbonize their operations and value chains — which directly reduces their exposure to transition risks such as carbon pricing, stranded asset risk, and loss of market access as low-carbon product standards tighten globally. From a supply chain perspective, the ripple effect of SBTi adoption is profound. European companies subject to the Corporate Sustainability Reporting Directive (CSRD) and its European Sustainability Reporting Standards (ESRS) are legally required to disclose and manage their Scope 3 emissions — which means they will increasingly demand that Indian suppliers either have SBTi-validated targets or provide verified emissions data that allows the buyer to calculate their own Scope 3 footprint. Indian exporters who cannot demonstrate credible decarbonization plans risk being delisted from global supply chains. The alignment with ISSB standards (IFRS S2 — Climate-related Disclosures) is equally significant. ISSB S2 requires companies to disclose their transition plans, climate targets, and progress — and an SBTi-validated target is widely accepted as evidence of a credible, science-aligned transition plan. SEBI's BRSR Core framework is itself progressively converging with ISSB standards, meaning SBTi adoption today will reduce compliance burden tomorrow. India's domestic climate policy trajectory — including the Carbon Credit Trading Scheme (CCTS) under the Energy Conservation (Amendment) Act 2022 — further reinforces the need for companies to establish a GHG baseline and reduction pathway now.</p><h3>What To Do Now</h3><ul><li>Establish Your GHG Baseline: Before committing to SBTi, companies must complete a robust GHG inventory across Scope 1, 2, and 3 emissions using the GHG Protocol Corporate Standard — engage a qualified third party to conduct or verify this inventory, as SBTi validation requires credible underlying data</li><li>Submit Your SBTi Commitment Letter: Register on the SBTi portal and submit a commitment letter to formally signal your intent — this starts the 24-month clock and publicly demonstrates climate ambition to investors, customers, and regulators</li><li>Identify Applicable SBTi Pathway and Sector Standard: Determine which SBTi sector-specific standard applies to your business (Corporate Standard, FLAG, Financial Institutions, or a sector pathway for steel/cement/apparel) and understand the specific decarbonization trajectory and coverage requirements for your industry</li><li>Map Scope 3 Hotspots and Engage Your Supply Chain: Conduct a Scope 3 screening to identify the most material upstream and downstream emission categories — prioritize supplier engagement, procurement policy changes, and product design reviews that will be required to meet Scope 3 target thresholds</li><li>Integrate SBTi into Board-Level Climate Governance: Ensure your Board or a dedicated ESG/Risk Committee formally owns the SBTi commitment — link executive remuneration to emissions reduction milestones, and embed SBTi progress into your annual BRSR and investor ESG reporting to demonstrate accountability</li></ul><h3>Our Take</h3><p>SBTi is no longer a nice-to-have badge for sustainability reports — it is rapidly becoming the baseline expectation for any Indian company with serious global ambitions, access to institutional capital, or exposure to European and US regulatory supply chain requirements. What makes the current moment particularly significant is the convergence of pressures: SEBI's BRSR Core is demanding verified climate data, CSRD is pushing Scope 3 accountability into Indian supply chains, the ISSB framework is being adopted by major trading partners, and India's own Carbon Credit Trading Scheme is creating domestic incentives for emissions accounting. Companies that invest in SBTi validation now are not just buying regulatory compliance — they are building the organizational capability in GHG accounting, transition planning, and stakeholder disclosure that will be table stakes for doing business by 2030. Our strong advice: treat SBTi not as a communications exercise but as a strategic transformation program, and start with the GHG inventory today.</p>

item.title: GRI Standards: The Global Baseline for Sustainability Reporting and What Indian Companies Must Know
item.description: The Global Reporting Initiative (GRI) Standards remain the world's most widely adopted framework for sustainability disclosure, providing a universal baseline against which stakeholders — investors, regulators, and supply chain partners — assess corporate ESG performance. For Indian companies, volun
item.content:encoded: <h3>Executive Summary</h3><p>The Global Reporting Initiative (GRI) Standards remain the world's most widely adopted framework for sustainability disclosure, providing a universal baseline against which stakeholders — investors, regulators, and supply chain partners — assess corporate ESG performance. For Indian companies, voluntary adoption of GRI is rapidly becoming a de facto requirement as SEBI's Business Responsibility and Sustainability Report (BRSR) framework converges with international norms and global customers demand transparent, comparable sustainability data. Understanding and aligning to the GRI Standards today is no longer optional — it is a strategic imperative for market access, investor confidence, and climate credibility.</p><h3>What Changed</h3><p>The GRI Standards are a modular, interoperable set of disclosure requirements developed by the Global Reporting Initiative, an independent international standards body. The Standards are structured in three tiers: Universal Standards (GRI 1, 2, and 3), which apply to all reporting organizations regardless of size or sector; Sector Standards, which provide tailored guidance for high-impact industries such as oil and gas, mining, agriculture, and financial services; and Topic Standards, which cover specific economic, environmental, and social issues such as climate change (GRI 305 — Emissions), water (GRI 303), labor practices, and human rights. In 2021, GRI completed a comprehensive overhaul of its Universal Standards, replacing the previous GRI 101, 102, and 103 with the current GRI 1 (Foundation), GRI 2 (General Disclosures), and GRI 3 (Material Topics) — effective for reports published on or after January 1, 2023. This update strengthened the concept of 'double materiality,' requiring companies to assess both how sustainability issues affect the business (financial materiality) and how the business impacts people and the planet (impact materiality). GRI also introduced a formal alignment with the UN Guiding Principles on Business and Human Rights, raising the bar on human rights due diligence disclosures. Critically, GRI Standards are now explicitly recognized by SEBI as a compatible framework for BRSR Core disclosures, making familiarity with GRI essential for any listed Indian company navigating the evolving mandatory reporting landscape.</p><h3>Who Is Affected</h3><ul><li>Listed Indian companies (especially NSE/BSE-listed entities in the top 1,000 by market capitalization) subject to SEBI's mandatory BRSR requirements, where GRI alignment supports Core disclosures</li><li>Export-oriented manufacturers and suppliers — particularly in textiles, chemicals, auto components, pharmaceuticals, and IT services — whose European and North American customers require GRI-aligned ESG reports</li><li>Indian subsidiaries of multinational corporations that must align with parent-company GRI reporting cycles and CSRD (Corporate Sustainability Reporting Directive) obligations in the EU</li><li>Financial institutions, banks, and asset managers responding to RBI and SEBI sustainability guidelines and investor ESG due diligence requirements</li><li>Infrastructure, energy, and mining companies covered by GRI Sector Standards, where sector-specific disclosures are increasingly scrutinized by lenders and multilateral agencies</li><li>SMEs in global supply chains where Tier-1 suppliers are being required to cascade GRI-aligned data requests to their own vendors</li></ul><h3>Key Obligations & Deadlines</h3><ul><li>Adopt GRI Universal Standards (GRI 1, 2, and 3) as the foundation of any sustainability report — mandatory for 'in accordance' reporting claims as of January 1, 2023</li><li>Conduct a formal double materiality assessment (GRI 3) that identifies and prioritizes material topics based on both impact on stakeholders and financial significance to the business — this must be documented and disclosed</li><li>Disclose against GRI 2: General Disclosures covering organizational profile, governance, strategy, stakeholder engagement, and reporting practices — these are non-negotiable for any GRI-aligned report</li><li>Identify applicable GRI Sector Standards (e.g., GRI 11 for Oil and Gas, GRI 12 for Coal, GRI 13 for Agriculture, Aquaculture and Fishing) and incorporate sector-specific disclosures — sector standards progressively becoming mandatory for 'in accordance' claims</li><li>Report Scope 1, 2, and 3 greenhouse gas emissions under GRI 305 (Emissions) using a recognized methodology such as the GHG Protocol — increasingly required by lenders, investors, and supply chain partners</li><li>Ensure human rights due diligence disclosures align with GRI 2-23 through 2-26 and relevant Topic Standards (GRI 406–413), reflecting the UN Guiding Principles alignment built into the 2021 update</li><li>For BRSR Core compliance (mandatory for top 150 listed companies from FY2023-24, expanding to top 1,000 by FY2026-27): cross-reference GRI disclosures to satisfy SEBI's ESG data requirements and minimize duplicative reporting burden</li><li>Obtain third-party assurance on GRI disclosures — a growing expectation from institutional investors and required for BRSR Core from FY2023-24 onwards for the top 150 listed entities</li></ul><h3>Climate Transition Angle</h3><p>GRI Standards sit at the center of the global convergence of sustainability reporting frameworks, making alignment a critical bridge between India's domestic regulatory requirements and international climate expectations. The International Sustainability Standards Board (ISSB) — whose IFRS S1 and S2 standards are now being considered for adoption in India — has explicitly built upon GRI's concept of impact materiality and cross-references GRI Topic Standards for non-financial disclosures. Companies that have a strong GRI reporting foundation will find the transition to ISSB-aligned reporting significantly smoother. From a physical climate risk perspective, GRI 201-2 (Financial Implications of Climate Change) and GRI 305 (Emissions) directly support the kind of climate risk disclosure now expected under TCFD-aligned frameworks that underpin both ISSB S2 and SEBI's BRSR. Indian companies in water-stressed sectors — agriculture, textiles, beverages — will find GRI 303 (Water and Effluents) increasingly scrutinized as physical water risk becomes a material financial issue. Similarly, supply chain disruptions linked to extreme weather events demand disclosure under GRI 204 (Procurement Practices) and GRI 308 (Supplier Environmental Assessment). For companies with European customers or investors, the EU's Corporate Sustainability Reporting Directive (CSRD) and its European Sustainability Reporting Standards (ESRS) are partially interoperable with GRI — meaning GRI disclosures can satisfy a significant portion of ESRS requirements, reducing the compliance burden for Indian exporters navigating the EU's value chain due diligence expectations.</p><h3>What To Do Now</h3><ul><li>Conduct a GRI Gap Analysis immediately: Map your current BRSR or sustainability disclosures against GRI Universal Standards (GRI 1, 2, and 3) to identify gaps in materiality assessment methodology, governance disclosures, and stakeholder engagement documentation</li><li>Formalize your Double Materiality Assessment: Convene a cross-functional team including Finance, Legal, Operations, and Sustainability to conduct and document a GRI 3-aligned materiality assessment — this is the backbone of a credible GRI report and increasingly a prerequisite for investor engagement</li><li>Build your GHG emissions inventory: If you have not already, initiate Scope 1 and Scope 2 measurement using the GHG Protocol, and begin mapping Scope 3 categories relevant to your sector — GRI 305 compliance and BRSR Core requirements both depend on this data</li><li>Identify your applicable GRI Sector Standard: Review GRI's published Sector Standards and determine which apply to your primary business activity; begin incorporating sector-specific disclosures into your next reporting cycle to stay ahead of the 'in accordance' requirements</li><li>Engage an assurance provider early: Third-party limited or reasonable assurance over GRI-aligned disclosures is becoming the market standard and is required for BRSR Core — identify a qualified assurance provider and integrate them into your reporting process from data collection, not just at the review stage</li></ul><h3>Our Take</h3><p>GRI Standards are no longer simply a voluntary best-practice framework for progressive companies — they are rapidly becoming the de facto infrastructure of mandatory sustainability disclosure in India and globally. SEBI's BRSR framework has effectively borrowed GRI's architecture, ISSB has built upon its foundation, and the EU's CSRD has formally recognized GRI as a compatible framework. For Indian companies, this convergence is both an opportunity and a warning signal: those who invest now in building robust GRI-aligned reporting systems will gain a significant competitive advantage in accessing global capital, winning export contracts, and demonstrating climate credibility. Those who delay risk being caught in a reactive compliance sprint — expensive, disruptive, and credibility-damaging. The direction of travel is unambiguous: comprehensive, assured, and comparable sustainability disclosure is the new cost of doing business in global markets, and GRI is the common language in which that disclosure must be spoken.</p>

item.title: EU CSRD and EFRAG's Evolving Standards: What Indian Companies Must Know About Europe's Sustainability Reporting Revolution
item.description: The European Union's Corporate Sustainability Reporting Directive (CSRD), underpinned by standards developed by the European Financial Reporting Advisory Group (EFRAG), is reshaping the global landscape of sustainability and financial reporting — and its reach extends well beyond European borders. I
item.content:encoded: <h3>Executive Summary</h3><p>The European Union's Corporate Sustainability Reporting Directive (CSRD), underpinned by standards developed by the European Financial Reporting Advisory Group (EFRAG), is reshaping the global landscape of sustainability and financial reporting — and its reach extends well beyond European borders. Indian companies that supply to, invest in, or operate within the EU market are increasingly subject to its disclosure requirements, either directly or through value chain obligations imposed on their European counterparts. Proactive alignment with CSRD and ESRS standards is no longer optional for globally integrated Indian businesses — it is a market access and investor confidence imperative.</p><h3>What Changed</h3><p>The EU's CSRD, which came into force in January 2023, replaces the older Non-Financial Reporting Directive (NFRD) and dramatically expands the scope, depth, and enforceability of sustainability disclosures for companies operating in or trading with the European Union. EFRAG, the technical body tasked with developing the European Sustainability Reporting Standards (ESRS), has published a comprehensive set of standards covering climate, environment, social, governance, and sector-specific topics that companies must report against. Unlike previous voluntary or high-level frameworks, ESRS mandates 'double materiality' — meaning companies must disclose not only how sustainability issues affect their financial performance (financial materiality), but also how their operations impact people and the planet (impact materiality). This dual lens fundamentally changes the nature of corporate sustainability disclosure from a reputational exercise to a rigorous, auditable financial reporting obligation. EFRAG continues to publish guidance, Q&amp;As, and implementation support materials to help companies navigate the complexity of ESRS. Sector-specific standards and standards for non-EU companies with significant EU exposure are also in development, signalling that the regulatory perimeter will continue to expand.</p><h3>Who Is Affected</h3><ul><li>Large EU-listed companies and large EU undertakings (balance sheet &gt; €20M, turnover &gt; €40M, or &gt;250 employees) — already in or entering scope from FY2024 onwards</li><li>Listed SMEs on EU regulated markets — phased in from FY2026 (with opt-out until 2028)</li><li>Non-EU parent companies with net turnover &gt; €150M in the EU and at least one large or listed subsidiary/branch in the EU — in scope from FY2028</li><li>Indian exporters and suppliers to EU-based companies — indirectly affected through value chain disclosure requirements imposed on their European customers</li><li>Indian companies with European institutional investors or dual-listed entities — subject to investor-driven disclosure expectations aligned with ESRS</li><li>Indian IT, pharma, textiles, automotive components, and chemicals sectors — among the highest-exposure industries given deep EU trade and investment ties</li><li>Indian conglomerates and multinationals with European subsidiaries — directly subject to CSRD obligations for those entities</li></ul><h3>Key Obligations & Deadlines</h3><ul><li>FY2024 (reports due 2025): Large EU public-interest entities (&gt;500 employees) already subject to NFRD must report under full ESRS for the first time — their value chain data requests to Indian suppliers begin NOW</li><li>FY2025 (reports due 2026): Other large EU companies (not previously under NFRD) enter scope — expanding the universe of EU buyers requesting ESRS-aligned sustainability data from Indian value chain partners</li><li>FY2026 (reports due 2027): Listed SMEs on EU regulated markets enter scope — further broadening supply chain disclosure pressure on Indian MSMEs</li><li>FY2028 (reports due 2029): Non-EU companies with €150M+ EU turnover and an EU subsidiary/branch must file a CSRD-compliant sustainability report — Indian multinationals should begin gap assessments immediately</li><li>Ongoing — EFRAG Sector-Specific Standards: EFRAG is developing sector ESRS (e.g., oil &amp; gas, mining, textiles, food &amp; agriculture); Indian companies in these sectors should monitor EFRAG's work programme closely</li><li>Ongoing — EFRAG Voluntary Standard for SMEs (VSME): Indian SMEs supplying EU customers can use EFRAG's VSME standard to respond to value chain data requests in a structured, proportionate way</li><li>Double Materiality Assessment: All in-scope companies must conduct and document a formal double materiality assessment — this is the foundational step before any ESRS disclosure can be prepared</li><li>Third-Party Assurance: CSRD requires limited assurance on sustainability statements from FY2024 reporters, with reasonable assurance expected to follow — Indian entities preparing consolidated reports must factor in audit readiness</li></ul><h3>Climate Transition Angle</h3><p>ESRS E1 — the climate change standard — is one of the most detailed and consequential components of the ESRS framework. It requires companies to disclose their transition plans, Scope 1, 2, and 3 greenhouse gas emissions, climate-related physical and transition risks, and alignment (or misalignment) with a 1.5°C pathway. This directly mirrors the recommendations of the Task Force on Climate-related Financial Disclosures (TCFD) and is closely interoperable with the ISSB's IFRS S2 Climate standard — creating a converging global architecture for climate financial disclosure. For Indian companies, the Scope 3 emissions requirement is particularly significant. EU companies must report upstream and downstream value chain emissions, meaning they will request primary emissions data from their Indian suppliers. Companies that cannot provide credible, verified Scope 3 data risk being excluded from EU supply chains or replaced by lower-carbon alternatives — a direct commercial consequence of climate disclosure gaps. India's own BRSR Core framework, mandated by SEBI for the top 150 listed companies from FY2023-24, shares conceptual DNA with ESRS — both require value chain disclosures, assurance, and climate risk reporting. Indian companies that invest in BRSR Core compliance are simultaneously building the capabilities needed to respond to CSRD-driven value chain requests. The convergence of BRSR, ISSB, and ESRS creates a strong strategic rationale for harmonised, integrated sustainability reporting infrastructure.</p><h3>What To Do Now</h3><ul><li>Map Your EU Exposure: Audit all EU revenue streams, customer relationships, investor bases, and subsidiary structures to determine whether your company faces direct CSRD obligations (as a non-EU parent) or indirect obligations (as a value chain partner) — and by when</li><li>Conduct a Double Materiality Assessment: Engage your sustainability and finance teams to identify which ESRS topics are material to your business under both the financial and impact lenses — this is the non-negotiable starting point for CSRD alignment and should be documented and defensible</li><li>Establish Scope 3 Data Collection Protocols: Begin collecting primary activity data across your supply chain to calculate and verify Scope 3 emissions — prioritise categories most relevant to your sector and most likely to be requested by EU customers under ESRS E1</li><li>Align BRSR Core and ESRS Reporting Infrastructure: Leverage your existing SEBI BRSR Core compliance programme as the foundation for CSRD readiness — identify gaps between BRSR disclosures and ESRS requirements, particularly on climate transition plans, biodiversity (ESRS E4), and workforce due diligence (ESRS S1)</li><li>Engage Legal and Assurance Partners Early: CSRD sustainability statements require third-party limited assurance — brief your statutory auditors or sustainability assurance providers now on ESRS requirements to avoid bottlenecks when reporting obligations crystallise</li></ul><h3>Our Take</h3><p>CSRD and the ESRS standards represent the most consequential shift in corporate reporting since the introduction of mandatory financial audits — and EFRAG's ongoing work to refine, expand, and enforce these standards means the regulatory surface area will only grow. For Indian business leaders, the temptation may be to treat CSRD as a 'European problem' that only affects subsidiaries or compliance teams in Brussels. That view is dangerously shortsighted. The value chain provisions, the non-EU parent company rules, and the sheer weight of EU institutional investor expectations mean that CSRD's gravitational pull will be felt in boardrooms across Mumbai, Bengaluru, and Chennai well before 2028. The companies that begin building double materiality frameworks, Scope 3 data pipelines, and assurance-ready disclosure processes today will not only comply — they will differentiate themselves as trusted, transparent partners in a global economy that is rapidly pricing climate and sustainability risk into every transaction.</p>

item.title: Behind the Rules: How ISSB Sets IFRS Sustainability Standards — And Why Indian Companies Must Pay Attention
item.description: The International Sustainability Standards Board (ISSB), operating under the IFRS Foundation, follows a rigorous, multi-stage due process to develop globally consistent sustainability disclosure standards — most notably IFRS S1 (General Requirements) and IFRS S2 (Climate-Related Disclosures). Unders
item.content:encoded: <h3>Executive Summary</h3><p>The International Sustainability Standards Board (ISSB), operating under the IFRS Foundation, follows a rigorous, multi-stage due process to develop globally consistent sustainability disclosure standards — most notably IFRS S1 (General Requirements) and IFRS S2 (Climate-Related Disclosures). Understanding how these standards are created is not merely academic: it gives Indian companies early warning of what is coming, allows them to engage in shaping the rules, and enables proactive alignment with frameworks that Indian regulators — including SEBI — are increasingly mirroring. Companies that track the ISSB's pipeline today will avoid costly compliance scrambles tomorrow.</p><h3>What Changed</h3><p>The IFRS Foundation, the independent not-for-profit body that oversees global accounting and sustainability reporting standards, has published a transparent account of how IFRS Standards — including those issued by the ISSB — are developed and maintained. The standard-setting process is structured, consultative, and iterative, designed to ensure that standards reflect the needs of investors, preparers, regulators, and the public across jurisdictions. At a high level, the process moves through several defined stages: agenda consultation (identifying priority topics), research and discussion papers (exploring potential approaches), exposure drafts (formal proposals open for public comment), standard finalisation (incorporating feedback and issuing the standard), and post-implementation review (assessing real-world effectiveness). Each stage includes mandatory public consultation periods, typically 90–120 days, during which any stakeholder — including Indian companies and regulators — can submit formal comments. For sustainability specifically, the ISSB has signalled that its near-term agenda beyond IFRS S1 and S2 will focus on biodiversity, human capital, and human rights disclosures. These are not distant abstractions — they are the next wave of mandatory reporting obligations that will cascade into Indian regulatory frameworks, supply chain contracts, and investor expectations within the next three to five years.</p><h3>Who Is Affected</h3><ul><li>Listed companies on Indian stock exchanges (BSE/NSE), particularly the top 1,000 by market capitalisation already subject to SEBI's Business Responsibility and Sustainability Report (BRSR) framework</li><li>Indian subsidiaries and joint ventures of multinational corporations headquartered in jurisdictions adopting ISSB standards (EU, UK, Japan, Singapore, Australia, and others)</li><li>Export-oriented manufacturers and suppliers integrated into global value chains where buyers are subject to CSRD or ISSB-aligned reporting obligations</li><li>Financial institutions — banks, asset managers, and insurers — increasingly required to disclose portfolio-level climate and sustainability risks</li><li>CFOs, Chief Sustainability Officers, and Investor Relations teams responsible for sustainability reporting strategy and investor communication</li><li>Audit and assurance firms advising on sustainability disclosure readiness and limited/reasonable assurance engagements</li></ul><h3>Key Obligations & Deadlines</h3><ul><li>IFRS S1 &amp; S2 (already issued, June 2023): Companies in jurisdictions that have adopted or endorsed these standards must disclose material sustainability-related risks and opportunities (S1) and climate-specific information including Scope 1, 2, and 3 emissions (S2) — early adopters began reporting in FY2024</li><li>SEBI BRSR Core (effective FY2023–24 onwards): India's BRSR Core framework is explicitly designed to be interoperable with ISSB standards; top 150 listed companies must provide assured BRSR Core disclosures — expand to top 250 by FY2024–25 and top 500 by FY2025–26</li><li>ISSB Agenda Consultation (ongoing): The ISSB periodically releases agenda consultations — monitor the IFRS Foundation website and submit responses to shape standards on biodiversity (TNFD-aligned), human capital, and just transition topics before they are finalised</li><li>Exposure Draft Comment Periods: When the ISSB releases exposure drafts (expected on biodiversity and human capital disclosures in 2025–2026), Indian companies have a 90–120 day window to submit formal comments — missing this window means losing the opportunity to influence rules that will affect you</li><li>ISSB Jurisdictional Adoption Tracker: Continuously monitor which of your key trading partners and investor-domicile countries have adopted ISSB standards, as contractual and investor-driven disclosure requirements will flow down to Indian entities regardless of domestic mandates</li></ul><h3>Climate Transition Angle</h3><p>The ISSB's standard-setting process is the architectural backbone of the global climate disclosure infrastructure. IFRS S2 is explicitly designed to be consistent with the Task Force on Climate-related Financial Disclosures (TCFD) recommendations and to interoperate with the EU's Corporate Sustainability Reporting Directive (CSRD) and its European Sustainability Reporting Standards (ESRS). This means that a standard shaped in Basel or London today becomes a supply chain requirement, investor screening criterion, or trade barrier for Indian companies within two to three years. From a physical risk perspective, IFRS S2 requires companies to disclose their exposure to acute events (cyclones, floods, heatwaves) and chronic shifts (sea-level rise, changes in precipitation) — risks that are acutely relevant to Indian industries from agriculture and textiles to infrastructure and financial services. The Ganges basin, coastal manufacturing clusters, and rain-fed agricultural supply chains all carry material physical climate risk that investors will soon demand to be quantified and disclosed under ISSB-aligned frameworks. The transition risk dimension is equally significant. As the ISSB refines requirements around carbon pricing exposure, stranded asset risk, and climate scenario analysis (using 1.5°C and 2°C pathways), Indian companies in carbon-intensive sectors — steel, cement, chemicals, thermal power — must build the internal data and modelling capabilities now to meet these disclosures credibly. Companies that treat ISSB alignment as a future problem will find themselves at a competitive and capital-access disadvantage against peers who have invested early.</p><h3>What To Do Now</h3><ul><li>Map your regulatory exposure: Identify which jurisdictions your investors, lenders, customers, and partners are based in, and determine whether those jurisdictions have adopted or are adopting ISSB standards — this maps your de facto compliance timeline even before Indian mandates expand</li><li>Subscribe to the ISSB's due process pipeline: Register on the IFRS Foundation website to receive notifications on new discussion papers and exposure drafts; assign a team member to review and respond to consultations on upcoming topics (biodiversity, human capital) — your response shapes standards that will govern your disclosures</li><li>Conduct a gap assessment against IFRS S1 and S2: Even if not yet mandated domestically, benchmark your current BRSR and ESG disclosures against IFRS S1 and S2 requirements to identify data gaps, particularly around Scope 3 emissions, climate scenario analysis, and governance disclosures</li><li>Invest in Scope 3 data infrastructure now: IFRS S2's Scope 3 requirements are the most technically demanding element — begin supplier engagement, primary data collection, and spend-based estimation methodologies immediately, as building this capability takes 18–24 months minimum</li><li>Brief your board and audit committee: Present the ISSB standard-setting timeline and its interoperability with SEBI BRSR Core to your board; ensure that sustainability disclosure is treated as a governance and financial reporting matter — not just a CSR or communications exercise</li></ul><h3>Our Take</h3><p>The ISSB's transparent, consultative standard-setting process is both a technical mechanism and a strategic opportunity — and most Indian companies are not yet using it as one. The window to influence rules on biodiversity disclosure, human capital metrics, and just transition reporting is open right now, through formal comment submissions and participation in ISSB roundtables and outreach events. Indian industry associations, the Institute of Chartered Accountants of India (ICAI), and SEBI itself have begun engaging with ISSB processes, but corporate-level participation remains thin. This is a missed opportunity. Beyond participation, the deeper significance of understanding how ISSB standards are made is this: the pipeline is predictable. Companies that track the research agenda today can build systems, train teams, and engage supply chains in advance — converting a compliance burden into a competitive advantage. The question for Indian business leaders is no longer whether ISSB-aligned disclosure will be required; it is whether you will be ready when it is.</p>

item.title: Batteries (Management and Handling) Rules, 2001: What Every Manufacturer, Importer, and Bulk Consumer Must Know
item.description: The Batteries (Management and Handling) Rules, 2001, notified under the Environment (Protection) Act, 1986, establish a comprehensive Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR) framework for lead acid batteries in India — placing binding obligations on every link in the battery value chain, from manufac
item.content:encoded: <h3>Executive Summary</h3><p>The Batteries (Management and Handling) Rules, 2001, notified under the Environment (Protection) Act, 1986, establish a comprehensive Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR) framework for lead acid batteries in India — placing binding obligations on every link in the battery value chain, from manufacturers and importers to dealers, recyclers, and bulk consumers. Companies that manufacture, import, assemble, recondition, sell, or use lead acid batteries in significant volumes are legally required to collect used batteries, channel them exclusively to registered recyclers, and file half-yearly compliance returns with State Pollution Control Boards. Non-compliance carries regulatory consequences including registration cancellation and environmental liability — risks that are increasingly scrutinised by investors and global supply chain partners.</p><h3>What Changed</h3><p>Originally notified on 16 May 2001 and subsequently amended — most significantly by S.O. 1002(E) dated 4 May 2010 — the Batteries (Management and Handling) Rules, 2001 created India's first structured take-back and recycling regime specifically for lead acid batteries. Lead acid batteries are classified as hazardous waste because they contain lead metal, a potent neurotoxin. Unregulated disposal and informal recycling of these batteries cause serious soil and groundwater contamination, as well as occupational health crises in recycling communities. The 2010 amendment tightened the framework in three important ways: it redefined 'bulk consumers' to explicitly include government departments such as Railways, Defence, Telecom, and Posts and Telegraph that purchase 100 or more batteries per year; it mandated that new batteries may only be sold to registered dealers; and it introduced a formal registration regime for importers with the Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB), including a five-year registration cycle and clear grounds for cancellation. Together, these rules create a closed-loop accountability system — every battery sold must be traceable back to a collection and recycling pathway. In plain terms: if your organisation touches lead acid batteries at any point — making them, importing them, selling them, or using large quantities of them — you have legal obligations under this framework. Ignorance of the rules is not a defence, and the regulatory net covers entities across the public and private sectors alike.</p><h3>Who Is Affected</h3><ul><li>Manufacturers of lead acid batteries and their components (factories and their CEOs/control persons)</li><li>Importers of new lead acid batteries or lead-containing components — must register with CPCB for a five-year period</li><li>Assemblers — entities that manufacture batteries by assembling components (partially exempt from certain obligations such as setting up collection centres)</li><li>Reconditioners — entities that repair and resell used lead acid batteries (similarly partially exempt)</li><li>Dealers — sellers and receivers of lead acid batteries on behalf of manufacturers, importers, assemblers, and reconditioners; must be registered to legally receive new batteries</li><li>Recyclers and registered recyclers — entities processing used batteries for lead recovery; must hold registration with MoEF or its designated agency</li><li>Auctioneers — entities conducting bulk sale of used batteries through tender, auction, or negotiation</li><li>Bulk consumers — any Central or State Government department, public sector undertaking, board, agency, or private company purchasing 100 or more batteries per annum (Railways, Defence, Telecom, Power utilities, large manufacturing plants, data centres, and telecom tower operators are prime examples)</li><li>Individual consumers — persons using lead acid batteries, subject to return obligations to dealers or designated collection centres</li><li>Original Equipment Manufacturers (OEMs) — manufacturers of equipment or products that use lead acid batteries as components (partially excluded from buy-back schedules)</li></ul><h3>Key Obligations & Deadlines</h3><ul><li>COLLECTION BUY-BACK TARGETS: Manufacturers, importers, assemblers, and reconditioners must collect used batteries back from the market in accordance with the prescribed Schedule — matched to the volume and type of new batteries sold (OEM and bulk consumer sales are excluded from this calculation).</li><li>REGISTERED RECYCLERS ONLY: All used batteries collected must be sent exclusively to recyclers registered with MoEF or its designated agency. Sending used batteries to unregistered informal recyclers is a direct regulatory violation.</li><li>HALF-YEARLY RETURNS — 30 JUNE &amp; 31 DECEMBER: Manufacturers, importers, assemblers, and reconditioners must file half-yearly returns in Form-I with the relevant State Pollution Control Board (SPCB), reporting sales volumes and buy-back performance. Deadlines are 30 June (for the first half of the year) and 31 December (for the second half) annually.</li><li>IMPORTER REGISTRATION WITH CPCB: All importers must register with the Central Pollution Control Board using Form-I for a five-year renewable period. Registration is contingent on compliance with collection targets and timely return filing. Non-compliance can lead to suspension or cancellation of registration.</li><li>CUSTOMS CLEARANCE CONDITION: Import of new lead acid batteries is contingent upon valid registration (with RBI and CPCB as applicable) — importers must ensure registration is current before initiating shipments.</li><li>COLLECTION CENTRES: Manufacturers, importers, and reconditioners must establish or jointly establish designated collection centres at various locations to receive used batteries from consumers and dealers.</li><li>SAFE TRANSPORTATION: Entities in the chain must ensure arrangements are in place with dealers for safe, environmentally sound transportation of used batteries from collection centres to registered recyclers — preventing environmental damage in transit.</li><li>SELL ONLY TO REGISTERED DEALERS: As of the 2010 amendment, new batteries may only be sold to registered dealers. Manufacturers and importers must verify dealer registration status before invoicing.</li><li>BUY RECYCLED LEAD FROM REGISTERED SOURCES ONLY: Manufacturers must source recycled lead exclusively from registered recyclers — closing the loop and preventing laundering of informally recycled material into the supply chain.</li><li>PUBLIC AWARENESS OBLIGATION: Manufacturers, importers, assemblers, and reconditioners must conduct public awareness campaigns — through advertisements, publications, or posters — covering: (a) the hazards of lead; (b) consumer return obligations; and (c) addresses of collection centres and dealers.</li><li>INTERNATIONAL RECYCLING SYMBOL: Batteries must carry the internationally recognised recycling symbol.</li><li>VIOLATION REPORTING: Manufacturers and importers must report dealer violations to the relevant SPCB or MoEF.</li><li>APPEALS: Appeals against CPCB orders on registration suspension or cancellation must be filed in writing within 30 days of the order, accompanied by a copy of the order being challenged.</li></ul><h3>Climate Transition Angle</h3><p>Lead acid batteries sit at a critical intersection of India's industrial base and its energy transition. Despite the rapid growth of lithium-ion technology, lead acid batteries remain the dominant chemistry for automotive starter batteries, telecom tower backup, industrial UPS systems, and rural electrification storage — and will continue to be for the foreseeable future. As India scales up renewable energy deployment, the demand for battery-based storage — including lead acid — is set to increase significantly before the transition to advanced chemistries becomes economically universal. From a physical risk perspective, unregulated lead acid battery disposal creates severe soil and groundwater contamination — particularly in peri-urban and industrial clusters. Companies with manufacturing or warehousing operations in regions with high informal recycling activity face direct physical and reputational risks, including contaminated water sources affecting operations and communities. Supply chain due diligence under emerging global frameworks is now scrutinising these upstream environmental impacts. Globally, the Batteries (Management and Handling) Rules mirror the direction of travel set by the European Union's Battery Regulation (2023), which mandates EPR schemes, carbon footprints, recycled content targets, and due diligence for battery supply chains — and is already affecting Indian exporters supplying components or finished products to European markets. India's domestic framework, while older and less granular on carbon metrics, establishes the foundational traceability and accountability infrastructure that ISSB (IFRS S2) and CSRD-aligned disclosures will increasingly require companies to evidence. Investors conducting ESG due diligence on Indian industrials, utilities, and auto sector companies are beginning to treat battery waste compliance as a proxy for broader environmental governance maturity. For companies on a net-zero trajectory, formalising battery waste management is not merely a compliance exercise — it is a prerequisite for credible circular economy claims, Scope 3 emissions accounting accuracy, and supply chain resilience in a resource-constrained world.</p><h3>What To Do Now</h3><ul><li>CONDUCT A VALUE CHAIN AUDIT: Map every entity in your battery supply chain — suppliers, dealers, recyclers — and verify their registration status with CPCB/MoEF. Immediately suspend transactions with unregistered dealers or recyclers and document your corrective actions.</li><li>ESTABLISH OR JOIN A COLLECTION NETWORK: If you are a manufacturer, importer, or reconditioner without a functioning collection centre network, explore joint collection centre arrangements with industry peers or authorised PRO (Producer Responsibility Organisation) networks to meet your take-back obligations cost-effectively.</li><li>SET UP A COMPLIANCE CALENDAR FOR HALF-YEARLY RETURNS: Assign ownership within your ESG or legal compliance team for Form-I return filing. Automate data collection on battery sales volumes and buy-back quantities to ensure accurate, timely submissions to the SPCB by 30 June and 31 December each year.</li><li>INTEGRATE BATTERY COMPLIANCE INTO ESG DISCLOSURES: If your organisation reports under BRSR (Business Responsibility and Sustainability Reporting), GRI, or is preparing for ISSB-aligned reporting, document your battery take-back rates, recycled lead sourcing percentages, and collection centre network coverage as part of your circular economy and waste management disclosures.</li><li>TRAIN PROCUREMENT AND OPERATIONS TEAMS: Ensure that procurement officers (especially in bulk consumer organisations — utilities, telecom, defence contractors, large manufacturers) understand their obligations under the Rules, including the requirement to return used batteries and the prohibition on disposing of batteries through informal channels. Embed battery waste handling into your HSE (Health, Safety, and Environment) standard operating procedures.</li></ul><h3>Our Take</h3><p>The Batteries (Management and Handling) Rules, 2001 represent India's foundational EPR architecture for hazardous battery waste — and while the framework is over two decades old, enforcement focus and stakeholder scrutiny are intensifying rather than diminishing. The rules are increasingly relevant in the context of India's battery storage ambitions, the PM-eBus Sadan programme, and the National Programme on Advanced Chemistry Cell Battery Storage, all of which will dramatically expand the battery economy and, consequently, the volume of end-of-life batteries requiring responsible management. Companies that have treated these rules as a low-priority compliance checkbox are exposed to both regulatory risk — registration cancellations, SPCB notices, and environmental liability — and reputational risk as global customers and investors apply tighter ESG supply chain standards. We expect the Central Pollution Control Board to continue tightening EPR targets and enforcement under the broader trajectory set by the Battery Waste Management Rules, 2022, which has already updated the framework for other battery chemistries. Organisations that build robust, auditable battery take-back and recycling systems now will be better positioned to absorb future regulatory tightening, meet global customer requirements, and make credible circular economy claims — turning a compliance obligation into a competitive and reputational advantage.</p>

item.title: India's Plastic Waste Management (Amendment) Rules, 2021: What Businesses Must Do Before the Deadlines Hit
item.description: India's Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change notified the Plastic Waste Management (Amendment) Rules, 2021 on 12 August 2021, introducing a phased ban on identified single-use plastic (SUP) items effective 1 July 2022, raising minimum thickness thresholds for plastic carry bags, and ex
item.content:encoded: <h3>Executive Summary</h3><p>India's Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change notified the Plastic Waste Management (Amendment) Rules, 2021 on 12 August 2021, introducing a phased ban on identified single-use plastic (SUP) items effective 1 July 2022, raising minimum thickness thresholds for plastic carry bags, and expanding Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR) obligations to brand owners and plastic waste processors. Companies across manufacturing, retail, food and beverage, FMCG, e-commerce, and packaging supply chains face hard legal deadlines — non-compliance carries significant regulatory and reputational risk. Acting now to audit plastic use, redesign packaging, and register under EPR frameworks is no longer optional; it is a board-level imperative.</p><h3>What Changed</h3><p>The Plastic Waste Management Rules, 2016 were originally notified to regulate the manufacture, use, collection, and recycling of plastic waste in India. The 2018 amendment tightened certain provisions, but the landmark 2021 amendment — drafted in March 2021 and finalised in August 2021 after public consultation — is the most sweeping revision to date. The amendment does four major things. First, it prohibits the manufacture, import, stocking, distribution, sale, and use of a defined list of single-use plastic items from 1 July 2022. The banned items include plastic sticks in ear buds, balloons, and flags; candy and ice-cream sticks; polystyrene (thermocol) decoration; cutlery such as plates, cups, glasses, forks, spoons, knives, straws, and trays; wrapping films around sweet boxes; PVC banners below 100 microns; and stirrers. Compostable plastic alternatives are explicitly exempted from some of these restrictions. Second, the rules raise the minimum thickness of plastic carry bags in two steps: to 75 microns from 30 September 2021, and further to 120 microns from 31 December 2022. Non-woven plastic carry bags must meet a minimum weight of 60 grams per square metre (GSM) from 30 September 2021. Third, the amendment broadens the scope of Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR) to explicitly include brand owners and plastic waste processors (recyclers, co-processors) alongside manufacturers and importers. This means brand owners — including FMCG companies and retailers who use plastic packaging — now carry direct legal accountability for end-of-life plastic management, not just the manufacturer of the packaging material. Fourth, the rules add new definitions for key terms: 'single-use plastic item,' 'non-woven plastic bag,' 'plastic waste processing,' 'thermoplastic,' and 'thermoset plastic.' These definitions matter because they set the legal boundaries of what is regulated.</p><h3>Who Is Affected</h3><ul><li>FMCG and Consumer Goods Companies: Any brand owner using plastic packaging — pouches, wrappers, multi-layer packaging — now falls directly under EPR obligations.</li><li>Food &amp; Beverage Industry: Restaurants, quick-service chains, packaged food manufacturers, and beverage companies that use cups, straws, cutlery, stirrers, or thermocol packaging face the SUP ban directly.</li><li>Retail and E-Commerce: Businesses using plastic carry bags, wrapping films, or SUP items in customer-facing or last-mile operations must transition their packaging and logistics.</li><li>Plastic Manufacturers and Importers: Must comply with revised thickness and GSM standards for carry bags and are now explicitly named in the EPR framework.</li><li>Plastic Waste Processors — Recyclers and Co-Processors: Newly brought within the regulatory ambit; must ensure compliance with amended rules and EPR guidelines.</li><li>Advertising and Event Management: PVC banners below 100 microns are banned; industries reliant on large-format outdoor advertising must switch materials.</li><li>Pharmaceutical and Healthcare Packaging: While primary pharma packaging has specific exemptions, secondary packaging using affected SUP items must be reviewed.</li><li>Importers of Any Category: Import of banned SUP items is prohibited alongside domestic manufacture, making customs compliance and supply chain sourcing a critical issue.</li></ul><h3>Key Obligations & Deadlines</h3><ul><li>30 September 2021 (PAST — remediation required if not done): Plastic carry bags must be at minimum 75 microns thick. Non-woven plastic carry bags must weigh at least 60 GSM. Companies still using sub-standard bags are in violation and must rectify immediately.</li><li>1 July 2022 (PAST — compliance audit required): Complete ban on manufacture, import, stocking, distribution, sale, and use of all identified SUP items (ear bud sticks, balloon sticks, plastic flags, candy sticks, ice-cream sticks, thermocol decoration, plastic cutlery, plates, cups, glasses, straws, trays, wrapping films around sweet boxes, invitation cards with plastic wrapping, cigarette packets with plastic wrapping, PVC banners below 100 microns, and stirrers). Companies must audit all operations to confirm no banned items remain in the supply chain.</li><li>31 December 2022 (PAST — compliance verification required): Plastic carry bags must be at minimum 120 microns thick. All non-compliant inventory must have been phased out by this date.</li><li>Ongoing — EPR Registration and Compliance: Brand owners must register with the Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB) and relevant State Pollution Control Boards under the EPR framework, submit annual returns on plastic packaging used and waste collected, and demonstrate fulfilment of EPR targets as notified by CPCB.</li><li>Ongoing — Labelling Requirements: Manufacturers, producers, and brand owners must ensure plastic carry bags and packaging materials bear the required markings including producer name, registration number, and thickness/GSM details as prescribed.</li><li>Ongoing — Plastic Waste Processors: Recyclers and co-processors must register under the amended rules and comply with guidelines issued by CPCB from time to time.</li><li>10-Year Sunset Clause: Any future notifications banning additional categories of carry bags, plastic sheets, covers made from plastic sheets, multi-layer packaging, or polystyrene and expanded polystyrene items will come into force 10 years from the date of this notification (i.e., 12 August 2031), giving industry a long-term transition horizon for more complex packaging formats.</li></ul><h3>Climate Transition Angle</h3><p>Plastic waste is not merely a waste management issue — it is a climate issue. The production of virgin plastic is a fossil fuel-intensive process, and single-use plastics that escape waste streams become a source of microplastic pollution with cascading effects on ecosystems, agriculture, and water security, all of which are physical climate risks that affect business continuity. India generates approximately 3.5 million tonnes of plastic waste annually, and a significant portion remains uncollected and unprocessed, contributing to soil degradation and water contamination that undermine agricultural supply chains. For companies aligning with global reporting frameworks, this regulation is directly material. The International Sustainability Standards Board (ISSB) IFRS S1 and S2 standards require companies to disclose material sustainability and climate risks — plastic waste management practices, EPR compliance, and exposure to single-use plastic bans are increasingly considered material risks for consumer goods, retail, and packaging companies. Similarly, the EU's Corporate Sustainability Reporting Directive (CSRD), which affects Indian subsidiaries and supply chain partners of European companies, requires disclosure on pollution, resource use, and circular economy practices, all of which are implicated by plastic waste obligations. The 2021 amendment also accelerates India's alignment with global momentum: over 60 countries have enacted SUP bans or restrictions, and the UN Global Plastics Treaty — currently under negotiation with India as a signatory participant — is expected to impose even more stringent obligations on plastic producers and brand owners globally. Companies that treat the 2021 amendment as a floor, not a ceiling, and invest in alternative materials, circular packaging design, and robust EPR infrastructure will be better positioned to manage tightening global requirements and access international markets where plastic-free credentials are increasingly a trade prerequisite.</p><h3>What To Do Now</h3><ul><li>Conduct a Full Plastic Inventory Audit: Map every plastic item used across your operations — product packaging, in-store consumables, logistics packaging, and marketing materials. Classify each item against the SUP ban list and thickness/GSM requirements. Identify gaps and non-compliant items that require immediate substitution or elimination.</li><li>Register Under the EPR Framework Immediately: If your company is a brand owner, manufacturer, importer, or plastic waste processor and has not yet registered with CPCB under the EPR portal (https://eprplastic.cpcb.gov.in), do so without delay. Assign a dedicated compliance officer responsible for annual EPR filings, target fulfilment, and documentation.</li><li>Engage Your Supply Chain: Issue formal compliance notices to all packaging suppliers, contract manufacturers, and logistics partners requiring them to confirm adherence to the minimum thickness (120 microns for carry bags) and GSM standards (60 GSM for non-woven bags). Include compliance clauses in procurement contracts and conduct supplier audits.</li><li>Accelerate Packaging Redesign and Alternative Sourcing: Work with your R&amp;D and procurement teams to identify and qualify alternative materials for any banned SUP items still in use. Prioritise compostable, reusable, or recyclable alternatives. Build a 12–18 month transition roadmap with clear milestones, factoring in consumer communication and any necessary product reformulation.</li><li>Integrate Plastic Compliance into ESG Reporting: Ensure your annual report, Business Responsibility and Sustainability Report (BRSR), and any ISSB- or GRI-aligned disclosures include quantitative data on plastic packaging used, EPR targets met, recycled content percentages, and progress toward eliminating SUP items. This positions your company credibly with investors, customers, and regulators as the UN Plastics Treaty tightens global norms.</li></ul><h3>Our Take</h3><p>The Plastic Waste Management (Amendment) Rules, 2021 represent a structural shift in how India regulates plastic — moving from a purely downstream, waste-collection model to upstream producer accountability through EPR, and from voluntary action to hard prohibitions on specific product categories. The SUP ban is not a symbolic gesture; enforcement actions by CPCB and State Pollution Control Boards have intensified since July 2022, and penalties under the Environment (Protection) Act, 1986 — including imprisonment and fines — apply to violations. What makes this amendment particularly significant for businesses is the explicit inclusion of brand owners in the EPR net: this means that even if your company does not manufacture plastic, if your brand appears on plastic packaging, you are legally responsible for its end-of-life management. Looking ahead, the 10-year sunset clause provides a transition window for complex packaging categories, but given the pace of the UN Plastics Treaty negotiations and India's stated ambition to eliminate single-use plastics entirely, companies that wait for the deadline before acting will face a costly, compressed transition. The businesses that will emerge strongest are those that treat this regulation as the catalyst for a circular economy transformation — redesigning products, building reverse logistics, and investing in recycled content — rather than a compliance checkbox to be minimally satisfied.</p>

item.title: GHG Protocol's Land Sector and Removals Standard: What Indian Businesses Need to Know About Nature-Based Carbon Accounting
item.description: The GHG Protocol's Land Sector and Removals Standard establishes the first comprehensive global framework for how companies must account for greenhouse gas emissions and carbon removals from land use, land-use change, forestry, and nature-based solutions. For Indian businesses — particularly those i
item.content:encoded: <h3>Executive Summary</h3><p>The GHG Protocol's Land Sector and Removals Standard establishes the first comprehensive global framework for how companies must account for greenhouse gas emissions and carbon removals from land use, land-use change, forestry, and nature-based solutions. For Indian businesses — particularly those in agriculture, food and beverages, plantations, real estate, and infrastructure — this standard fundamentally changes how carbon sequestration claims and land-related emissions must be measured, reported, and verified. Companies that fail to align with this standard risk having their net-zero commitments and carbon offset claims dismissed by investors, regulators, and global supply chain partners.</p><h3>What Changed</h3><p>Until now, the GHG Protocol's existing corporate accounting standards — most notably the Corporate Standard (2001) and the Land Use, Land-Use Change and Forestry (LULUCF) guidance — provided only partial and inconsistent direction on how companies should account for carbon stored in, or released from, land. This left a significant gap: companies could make sweeping claims about forest conservation, agroforestry, or mangrove restoration as carbon 'removals' without a rigorous, standardised methodology underpinning those claims. The Land Sector and Removals Standard closes that gap. It provides detailed accounting rules for biogenic carbon (carbon stored in living biomass, soil, and organic matter), land-based carbon removal activities (such as afforestation, soil carbon sequestration, and wetland restoration), and the treatment of carbon dioxide removals (CDRs) in corporate inventories and net-zero targets. Critically, the standard distinguishes between emission reductions (doing less harm) and actual removals (actively drawing down CO2), a distinction that has enormous implications for corporate net-zero integrity. It also introduces rules on how to treat permanence risk — the possibility that stored carbon is re-released due to fire, disease, or land conversion — and how to handle reversals in corporate accounts.</p><h3>Who Is Affected</h3><ul><li>Agribusiness and food companies with supply chains involving crops, livestock, or forest commodities (e.g., tea, coffee, palm oil, timber, rubber)</li><li>Plantation and forestry companies managing large tracts of land for commercial or conservation purposes</li><li>Fast-moving consumer goods (FMCG) companies sourcing agricultural raw materials and making 'net-zero' or 'carbon neutral' product claims</li><li>Real estate and infrastructure developers undertaking land-use change, especially in peri-urban or ecologically sensitive zones</li><li>Energy companies investing in biomass, biogas, or biofuel feedstocks tied to land use</li><li>Financial institutions and ESG-focused investors financing land-sector projects or companies with significant land-related emission profiles</li><li>Companies purchasing voluntary carbon offsets from nature-based solutions (NbS) projects — such as REDD+, afforestation, or blue carbon — to meet climate targets</li><li>Any company with a Science Based Target (SBTi) or net-zero commitment that relies on land-sector removals to achieve balance</li></ul><h3>Key Obligations & Deadlines</h3><ul><li>Separate accounting for emissions and removals: Companies must now disaggregate land-sector emissions from removals in their GHG inventories rather than netting them out — ensuring transparency and preventing double-counting.</li><li>Biogenic carbon boundary setting: Organisations must clearly define the boundary of biogenic carbon flows in their inventory, including CO2 absorbed by crops, forests, or soils within their operational or value chain scope.</li><li>Permanence and reversal accounting: Companies must assess and disclose the permanence risk of any carbon stored through land-based activities and establish protocols for how reversals (e.g., a forest fire releasing stored carbon) will be reflected in future inventories.</li><li>Distinction between CDR and emission reductions in net-zero targets: Net-zero claims must separately quantify how much of the 'balance' comes from genuine removals versus avoided emissions — aligning with the Science Based Targets initiative's (SBTi) Corporate Net-Zero Standard.</li><li>Supply chain (Scope 3) land emissions: Companies must account for land-use change emissions embedded in their supply chains — for instance, deforestation linked to soy, palm oil, or cattle — under Scope 3, Category 1 (purchased goods and services).</li><li>Voluntary carbon market alignment: Offsets from nature-based solutions used in corporate claims must meet the standard's requirements for additionality, permanence, and accurate quantification — raising the bar for which credits qualify.</li><li>Formal deadline for adoption: The GHG Protocol has not yet mandated a universal compliance deadline; however, alignment is expected to be required by major frameworks (SBTi, ISSB, CDP) as they update their guidance. Companies should treat 2025–2026 as the de facto adoption window for leading practice.</li></ul><h3>Climate Transition Angle</h3><p>India's land sector is both a major source of emissions and one of the country's most significant potential carbon sinks. India's Nationally Determined Contribution (NDC) under the Paris Agreement explicitly commits to creating an additional carbon sink of 2.5 to 3 billion tonnes of CO2 equivalent through forest and tree cover by 2030.
